title: Why Propagate Alone? Parallel Use of Labels and Features on Graphs

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.
reviews.main_review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 1: The contributions are neither significant nor novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.

Main Review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?

Summary Of The Review:
I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Paper Summary

This work investigates the "label trick," a technique that facilitates concurrent propagation of labels alongside features and has become instrumental in several state-of-the-art GNN models. Despite its widespread adoption, the label trick has lacked rigorous theoretical grounding. This paper addresses that gap by providing a formal theoretical treatment. The authors propose a self-excluded variant through an extreme partitioning approach, demonstrate that random partitioning schemes employed in the label trick function as a form of regularization on self-excluded label parameters, and explore broader potential applications including: (i) augmenting graph-based methods with learnable parameters, and (ii) eliminating stochasticity through incorporation of self-excluded propagation into GNNs built on linear propagation modules.

## Detailed Comments

**(1) Limited Empirical Validation of Proposed Applications**

The authors appropriately choose not to re-establish the label trick's effectiveness, which has been well-documented in prior literature. Instead, they pivot toward demonstrating novel application scenarios outlined in Section 4. While this strategic choice is reasonable, the experimental results presented in Sections 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4 provide limited evidence that the label trick delivers meaningful improvements in these new contexts. Across numerous experimental settings, the performance gains appear marginal or negligible when accounting for variance across the ten experimental replicates. The empirical evidence does not convincingly support the value proposition of these proposed use cases.

**(2) Insufficient Characterization of the Label Trick's Practical Impact**

The paper's justification for the label trick's prominence relies on a somewhat cursory observation: "For instance, at the time of submission, the top 10 leaderboard positions across various teams in the KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC all employ the label trick, as do the top 3 overall results." While this observation is noteworthy, it does not establish a causal link between the label trick and superior performance. To substantiate such a claim rigorously, one would require a controlled ablation study that compares leading Challenge models both with and without the label trick applied. Furthermore, the review raises an important question: are there alternative mechanisms driving the label trick's adoption, such as accelerated convergence, improved training stability, or enhanced robustness? The paper would benefit from investigating these potential benefits beyond raw performance metrics.

## Overall Assessment

The paper makes a valuable contribution through its formal theoretical framework and analysis of the label trick. Nevertheless, the work is hampered by two significant limitations: the proposed novel applications motivated by the theoretical insights demonstrate only weak empirical benefits, and the analysis of what makes the label trick practically valuable remains incomplete and underdeveloped.

## Recommendation

**6: marginally above the acceptance threshold**

The theoretical contributions are sufficiently solid to warrant publication, though the practical impact and experimental validation require strengthening.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors provide a thorough theoretical justification to the recently emerging and heuristically proposed label trick, i.e., adopting a random portion of node labels in the training set as the input node attributes. Specifically, the authors start from the simplest case of label trick and derive the general formulation of the label trick as a regularizer. Motivated by the theoretical analysis, the authors proposed novel use cases of the label trick in broader scenarios.
reviews.main_review: The work is solid with rigorous formulation, theoretical justification, and novel use cases of the label trick. The authors derive general formulations for the label trick as regularizations in both regression and classification cases. The derivation is presented in a smooth way and provides several inspiring and insightful remarks and conclusions. The proposed use cases based on the theoretical are interesting and promising according to the empirical evaluations. Overall, I recommend acceptance due to the thorough analysis and potentially broader use cases inspired by the work. However, my assessment might be inaccurate due to my limited expertise in this topic.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: A solid and inspiring theoretical work. Novel use cases are introduced based on theoretical grounding and are justified by promising empirical results.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 4: The contributions are significant, and do not exist in prior works.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 2: You are willing to defend your assessment, but it is quite likely that you did not understand the central parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors provide a thorough theoretical justification to the recently emerging and heuristically proposed label trick, i.e., adopting a random portion of node labels in the training set as the input node attributes. Specifically, the authors start from the simplest case of label trick and derive the general formulation of the label trick as a regularizer. Motivated by the theoretical analysis, the authors proposed novel use cases of the label trick in broader scenarios.

Main Review:
The work is solid with rigorous formulation, theoretical justification, and novel use cases of the label trick. The authors derive general formulations for the label trick as regularizations in both regression and classification cases. The derivation is presented in a smooth way and provides several inspiring and insightful remarks and conclusions. The proposed use cases based on the theoretical are interesting and promising according to the empirical evaluations. Overall, I recommend acceptance due to the thorough analysis and potentially broader use cases inspired by the work. However, my assessment might be inaccurate due to my limited expertise in this topic.

Summary Of The Review:
A solid and inspiring theoretical work. Novel use cases are introduced based on theoretical grounding and are justified by promising empirical results.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary of the Paper

This paper offers a comprehensive theoretical foundation for the label trick—a recently proposed heuristic technique that incorporates randomly selected node labels from the training set as input node features. The authors begin with the most basic instantiation of this approach and progressively develop a generalized framework that characterizes the label trick through the lens of regularization. Building on these theoretical insights, the authors introduce several novel applications of the label trick across different problem settings.

## Main Review

This manuscript presents a well-executed contribution combining rigorous mathematical formulation with solid theoretical grounding and practical extensions of the label trick. The authors develop general frameworks that characterize the label trick as a regularization mechanism for both regression and classification tasks. The mathematical derivations are presented clearly and coherently, yielding several valuable observations and interpretable conclusions. The practical applications emerging from this theoretical analysis demonstrate considerable promise, as evidenced by the empirical results presented. The work successfully bridges theory and practice, offering both conceptual clarity and actionable insights. I recommend acceptance based on the depth of the theoretical analysis and the potential for the proposed extensions to find broader application. That said, I acknowledge that my assessment could be limited by my familiarity with this particular research area.

## Summary of the Review

A well-grounded theoretical contribution with solid mathematical rigor. The paper introduces theoretically motivated applications with encouraging empirical validation.

## Rating

**8: accept, good paper**

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.

Main Review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)

Summary Of The Review:
The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Paper Summary

This work investigates how label information can be leveraged within graph neural networks (and alternatively, how node features function in classical collective classification approaches such as label propagation) for the purpose of node-level prediction tasks. The authors examine the stochastic "label trick," which involves augmenting node feature representations by appending training labels and subsequently applying random masking to circumvent degenerate solutions. Under certain simplifying conditions (specifically, the absence of node features), they demonstrate that this procedure reduces to an objective function comprising a fitting component and a regularization component. This theoretical insight motivates the development of several practical algorithmic contributions.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

**Strengths (+)**

- The core research question is timely and significant. Bridging the conceptual gap between classical label propagation techniques and contemporary message-passing neural network architectures represents a valuable direction for the research community.

- The motivation is compelling. The authors identify a genuine limitation of the label trick—namely, the tendency toward trivial identity mapping solutions—and propose an elegant remedy through selective inclusion of off-diagonal propagation matrix entries while excluding diagonal components (which remain appropriate for feature propagation). Equation (10) provides clear intuition for this design choice.

- The investigation of the label trick is scientifically sound. The authors correctly observe that this technique has primarily served as an effective heuristic without rigorous justification, and deeper analysis of its properties merits scholarly attention.

**Weaknesses (–)**

- Attribution should be more carefully handled. The referenced work (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf) appears to merit greater acknowledgment regarding its role in introducing the label trick methodology.

- The conceptual foundation of Equation (3) requires clarification. The motivation for representing the column space of $Y_{tr}$ through post-multiplication by a learnable weight matrix $W$ is not sufficiently explained. This concern extends to the trainable smoothing mechanism within the Correct & Smooth framework, where similar design choices lack clear justification.

- Given the theoretical orientation of this manuscript, engagement with related literature is incomplete. The paper should reference and compare against existing work examining label versus feature propagation mechanisms (even when applied independently rather than in concert), particularly Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

- Mathematical exposition requires improvement. Notation becomes difficult to follow in places, particularly the subscript notation $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ which lacks sufficient clarity. Equation 5 warrants detailed explanation, especially the derivation of the final term from the preceding expression. Given that $D_{out}$ has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be computed over a discrete uniform distribution over $i \in \{1 \dots n\}$, yielding $\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i )$? The current presentation leaves this ambiguity unresolved. More broadly, the dense notational structure (particularly excessive subscripting) and the fragmented presentation through numerous remarks compromise readability. A comprehensive revision addressing clarity would strengthen the manuscript considerably.

- Evaluation methodology in Table 2 is inappropriate. The $R^2$ coefficient should be employed for regression tasks rather than the currently reported metric.

- Section 4.1 lacks sufficient detail and clarity. The training procedure for the proposed method should be explicitly described. What constitutes the final trained model—is it essentially a learned weight matrix applied to a Neumann series expansion in place of a fixed propagation matrix? Additionally, the paper should acknowledge and discuss related work on learning or modifying adjacency and propagation matrices.

## Questions for Authors

- Why is the deterministic variant of the label trick excluded from the comparison shown in the right panel of Figure 1? Additionally, the specific GNN architecture employed appears unspecified in Section 5.3—this should be clarified.

- In Remark 4, the claim that disabling regularization eliminates overfitting risk requires justification. What mechanism prevents overfitting in this scenario?

- The derivation demonstrates that the deterministic case admits decomposition with a regularization term. Are these theoretical findings applied to generate the results in Table 3? If not (which appears to be the case from the text), the rationale for this omission should be explained.

## Minor Issues

- Remark 2 contains a spelling error: "insure" should read "ensure"
- Inconsistent notation between Equations (4) and (5): parentheses and square brackets are used interchangeably where consistency would be preferable

## Overall Assessment

The paper addresses a problem of genuine importance and demonstrates promising theoretical insights. However, the work requires substantial refinement before publication. The issues outlined above—particularly concerning mathematical clarity, attribution, comparative analysis, and experimental design—must be addressed. A thorough revision would significantly improve the manuscript's quality and impact.

## Recommendation

**Rating: 5 (marginally below the acceptance threshold)**

The paper shows merit but currently falls short of the acceptance bar due to the cumulative effect of the concerns identified above. With careful attention to the revision points outlined in this review, this work has potential for acceptance at a future submission.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper focus on the label trick.
The label tricks is a method for graph-based semi-supervised learning.
In this setting, we have some labeled data points and some unlabeled data points, the goal is to label the unlabeled data.
The data points can form vertices of a graph, with edges built based on similarity of the points (eventually with a weighting scheme).
Labels can be diffused along the graph to label unlabeled data thanks to graph Laplacian.
A other way to leverage a graph structure is to build a graph neural networks and learn parameters of this networks to match labels of labeled data.
The authors states that the first method is utilizing labels and the second method is utilizing features based on a graphical model. They say that both should be done simultaneously, and argue that this is what the label trick is doing.

Eventually known labels can used along with features as the input of a GNN, but if we only ask to match labels of labeled data, than a trivial mapping can be learnt (the identity from known labels to the labels of labeled data). Therefore it is smart to use a drop-out strategy where we hide few labels (in particular the ground-truth label) when we want to learn the labels of a labeled data. This is the label trick.

The paper tries to understand what the label trick is about by looking at simple models.
It also discussed on its novel-case of applications.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- It is nice to take a step back to tries to understand what might look like wizard engineering, and show that the labels tricks can be understood with some simple models.
- This paper is relatively easy to follow and well-written

Weaknesses:
- This results in this paper are not really satisfactory. The background and motivations are not really well explained. And the duality between stochasticity of the label trick and the deterministic regularization is not really back-up by "to-the-point" experiments. 
- The paper is lacking rigor as I will detail later.
- Some claims of the paper are weird to me, in particular, the way the graph is built should indeed use features knowledge. So graph-based Laplacian do leverage features (yet in an unsupervised way). Also if the node features are noisy or unreliable, which will downgrade the performance of GNN, I believe the graph structure than will also be noisy or unreliable for those nodes if the graph is build based on features similarity.
- For a paper that seeks a theoretical explanation of the usefulness of the label trick. I do not have the impression to have learn a lot from it. For example, why self-excluded prediction do not work as well as full execution of the label trick?
Also, why the label trick is better than GNN with a Laplacian regularization akin Pfau et al. or Cabannes et al. with a MixUp regularization akin Lopez-Paz et al.?


Notes

page 1:
1/ The readibility of the paper would be improved if the authors were to go straight to the point and more rigorous. For example of “straight to the point", at line 4 in the introduction, the fact that labels and features vary smoothly over the graph and i.i.d. are two differents problems. And talking about i.i.d. samples is only adding confusion in the mind of the reader (at least in my mind).
For example of “rigorous", in the abstract, talking about the identity mapping from input to output, I believe the authors talks about the transductive setting, in which the feature X is forgotten but a propagation rule is used, which can be null as explained for label leakage in Eq. (3). Clearly the "identity mapping" from inputs in R^d to outputs in R^c is not well defined.

page 2:
1/ It would be nice to explain more clearly the graph formalism. For example:
Each node is linked to a input point x in R^d, and a label in R^c, from which we can form the node feature matrix (or design matrix) X and the label matrix of the nodes (or label matrix) Y. The graph is a undirected and edge have weights. The adjacency matrix is the matrix of weights (symmetric because the graph is undirected), thus S is symmetric. 

2/ "Nodes connected by an edge […] are likely to share the same labels". If the graph is weighted this depend on the weight. If the graph is unweighted, it should be specified.
 
3/ Eq.(1) I believe the second F should be F_tr, otherwise, there is, using pythagoras a penalization pushing F towards zeros on testing data. A close form solution can still be written with this correct formulation as well as the power method. The fact that Y = 0 is an informative labels (e.g. because Y are logits) and that pushing F towards 0 does not bias the system should be specified.
Also it would be nice to specify F \in R^{n \times c}, and to specify also that this minimizing this objective can be done independently according to each coordinates of \Y = \R^c, if there is no constraints on F.

page 3:
1/ There is a mistake with P \approx PY on the second paragraph. 
Also the approximation can be written as an equality with an inifinite sum since \lambda S is contracting (equality with a series being here understood as the convergence in any norm).

2/ "where […] P smooths the training labels” is weird since P is not used to defined F^(k). This observation should be written after the definition of P = (1 - \lambda)(I - \lambda S)^{-1}.

page 4:
1/ "model from (…)”: the parenthesis should be removed

2/ A note on SGC would be highly appreciated, as well as an explanation on the relevance of such a model. Space can be found by condensing section 3.2. and 3.3.

3/ What is P in Eq.(3), P was defined as “a propagation matrix”, how is it fixed here?

reviews.text:
# Peer Review (Rewritten)

## Summary of the Paper

This work examines the label trick, a technique employed in graph-based semi-supervised learning contexts. The setting involves a collection of labeled and unlabeled data points that form the vertices of a graph, where edges are constructed according to similarity measures between points and potentially weighted accordingly. The authors distinguish between two approaches: (1) label propagation via graph Laplacian, which leverages the graph structure itself, and (2) graph neural networks, which exploit node features through a learned graphical model. The paper argues that an effective approach should simultaneously employ both strategies. To prevent trivial solutions when training on labeled data (where a simple identity mapping from known labels to predictions would suffice), the authors propose using a dropout mechanism that randomly masks labels during training. This masking strategy constitutes the core of the label trick. The paper attempts to provide theoretical insight into this technique through analysis of simplified models and explores potential novel applications.

## Strengths

- The paper makes a valuable contribution by stepping back from what might appear to be an ad-hoc engineering solution and demonstrating that the label trick can be understood through the lens of simpler, more interpretable models.
- The presentation is generally accessible and well-organized, making the content reasonably easy to follow.

## Weaknesses

- **Insufficient theoretical justification**: The results presented do not adequately support the paper's claims. The background motivation lacks clarity, and critically, the proposed duality between the stochastic nature of the label trick and deterministic regularization approaches is not convincingly demonstrated through targeted experimental validation.

- **Rigor and clarity issues**: The paper exhibits several problems with mathematical and conceptual rigor that I detail below.

- **Questionable claims**: Several assertions merit scrutiny. Most notably, the claim that graph-based Laplacian methods do not leverage features is problematic—graph construction inherently depends on feature information, albeit applied in an unsupervised manner. Furthermore, if node features are noisy or unreliable (which would degrade GNN performance), then any graph built on feature similarity would likewise be noisy or unreliable for those same nodes, creating a logical inconsistency in the argument.

- **Limited explanatory power**: For a paper purporting to offer theoretical understanding of the label trick's effectiveness, the insights provided are disappointingly shallow. The work fails to address fundamental questions: Why does self-excluded prediction underperform relative to the full label trick implementation? How does the label trick compare theoretically and empirically to alternative approaches such as graph Laplacian regularization (as in Pfau et al. or Cabannes et al.) or mixup-based regularization strategies (as in Lopez-Paz et al.)?

## Detailed Comments

**Page 1:**
1. The presentation would benefit from greater directness and mathematical precision. For instance, the claim at line 4 that label and feature smoothness over the graph differs from i.i.d. assumptions is true, but invoking i.i.d. language introduces unnecessary confusion. The concepts should be clearly separated.

2. In the abstract, the reference to an "identity mapping" from inputs to outputs requires clarification. This appears to reference the transductive setting where features are discarded in favor of a propagation rule (which may be null, as shown in Eq. 3). An identity mapping from R^d to R^c is not well-defined in this context and needs explicit treatment.

**Page 2:**
1. The graph formalism deserves more precise exposition. Specifically, clarify that each node corresponds to an input point x ∈ R^d and a label in R^c, forming a node feature matrix X and label matrix Y. Specify that the graph is undirected with weighted edges, where the adjacency matrix S is symmetric.

2. The statement "Nodes connected by an edge [...] are likely to share the same labels" is weight-dependent in the weighted case and requires explicit specification for the unweighted case.

3. In Eq. (1), the second F should be F_tr; otherwise, by the Pythagorean theorem, the objective penalizes F toward zero on test data. The closed-form and power method solutions remain valid under this correction, but the paper must clarify that Y = 0 represents informative labels (e.g., logits) and that this regularization does not introduce bias. Additionally, specify that F ∈ R^{n×c} and note that the objective can be minimized independently for each coordinate of Y ∈ R^c when F is unconstrained.

**Page 3:**
1. The approximation P ≈ PY in the second paragraph contains an error. This relationship can be written as an exact equality using an infinite series, since λS is contracting; convergence should be understood in an appropriate norm.

2. The phrase "where [...] P smooths the training labels" is awkwardly positioned, as P is not used to define F^(k). This observation should appear after P = (1 - λ)(I - λS)^{-1} is formally introduced.

**Page 4:**
1. Remove unnecessary parentheses in "model from (...)".

2. A discussion of SGC and its relevance to the analysis would strengthen the paper. Space constraints could be addressed by condensing Sections 3.2 and 3.3.

3. Clarify the role of P in Eq. (3). Since P was previously defined as a propagation matrix, explain how it is fixed or determined in this context.

**Appendix:**
Several overfull boxes appear in the typeset output; please review the LaTeX compilation log and correct these formatting issues.

**References:**
Update reference formatting to cite journal and conference versions of works rather than arXiv preprints where available.

## Summary

While the motivation to provide post-hoc justification for practical techniques is commendable, this paper falls short of delivering a satisfactory theoretical analysis of the label trick. Most critically, the work does not rigorously establish why the label trick outperforms competing approaches. The research direction is promising and merits continued development, but the current execution requires substantial improvements before it meets publication standards.

## Rating

**5: marginally below the acceptance threshold**

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes a deterministic version for the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), that is based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process in order to avoid label leakage. The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. reviews.main_review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Some fundamental issues in the paper are preventing me from accepting this paper: the experimental validation is insufficient and unconvincing. The motivation for proposing the deterministic alternative to the label trick is not discussed.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes a deterministic version for the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), that is based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process in order to avoid label leakage. The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. Experiments are shown to compare with regular label propagation, with and without label trick, effect of regularization term in the deterministic setting vs randomized one.

Main Review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?

Summary Of The Review:
Some fundamental issues in the paper are preventing me from accepting this paper: the experimental validation is insufficient and unconvincing. The motivation for proposing the deterministic alternative to the label trick is not discussed.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Paper Summary

This work introduces a deterministic alternative to the stochastic label trick commonly used in graph neural networks and related graph-based methods. The proposed approach employs a self-exclusion mechanism within the propagation framework to mitigate label leakage issues. The authors explore potential applications across various label propagation contexts and provide experimental comparisons examining the deterministic variant against standard label propagation with and without the label trick, as well as analyses of regularization effects in both deterministic and stochastic settings.

## Strengths

1. The paper presents a novel deterministic framework that replaces the conventional stochastic label trick through an exclusion-based propagation mechanism. This conceptual contribution appears to offer a fresh perspective on handling label propagation in GNNs.

2. Theorem 2, which establishes an upper bound on the deterministic proposal under cross-entropy loss, represents a valuable theoretical contribution that helps ground the approach mathematically.

## Weaknesses

1. **Insufficient Motivation**: The justification for transitioning from a stochastic label trick to a deterministic one remains unclear. Beyond addressing sampling concerns, what computational or practical advantages does the deterministic approach provide? This fundamental question is inadequately addressed.

2. **Unclear Advantages**: While the authors contend that prior label trick applications lack theoretical grounding, the manuscript fails to articulate concrete benefits of the deterministic alternative in terms of accuracy, computational efficiency, or other measurable metrics compared to existing approaches.

3. **Inadequate Experimental Comparison**: The experimental section is problematic in its design. Apart from Figure 1, there is a notable absence of direct empirical comparisons between the proposed deterministic method and state-of-the-art stochastic label trick implementations. Instead, the experiments focus on tangential comparisons—such as trainable versus non-trainable variants and experiments with versus without the label trick—that do not directly validate the core contribution.

4. **Unconvincing Empirical Results**: Even within the experiments that do address trainable label propagation and the label trick itself, the results lack persuasive power. The observed improvements between trainable and traditional label propagation approaches, as well as the benefits of incorporating the label trick, are often marginal or negligible when accounting for standard deviation across trials. This raises questions about the practical significance of the proposed method.

5. **Missing Key References**: The paper overlooks important prior work in the field. Specifically, "Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes" by Szlam, Maggioni, and Coifman (2008, JMLR 9(55):1711−1739) should be discussed and incorporated into the related work.

6. **Clarity and Completeness Issues**: Several algorithmic descriptions lack sufficient detail and self-contained exposition, making it difficult for readers to fully grasp the methods. Additionally, important notation such as the reference to C&S remains unexplained.

## Summary Assessment

The paper faces several fundamental obstacles that prevent acceptance. Most critically, the experimental validation is insufficient and fails to convincingly demonstrate the value of the proposed deterministic approach. Equally important, the underlying motivation for preferring a deterministic over a stochastic label trick is inadequately articulated. These issues must be resolved before the work can make a compelling contribution to the field.

## Recommendation

**Rating: 5 (marginally below the acceptance threshold)**

1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?

Summary Of The Review:
Some fundamental issues in the paper are preventing me from accepting this paper: the experimental validation is insufficient and unconvincing. The motivation for proposing the deterministic alternative to the label trick is not discussed.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Imitation Learning by Reinforcement Learning

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper contributes a theoretical analysis of imitation learning (IL) under deterministic experts. The paper shows that IL in this setting can be reduced to RL with a stationary reward, and the stationary reward minimizes the total variation distance between the expert and the learner. Several empirical experiments were presented to validate the theoretical analysis.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
1. The paper shows that imitation learning (IL) may be reduced to RL with stationary reward for deterministic experts. The analysis provides the theoretical grounding for several existing IL methods. The paper also contributes the connection between the stationary reward to minimizing f-divergence typically seen in adversarial IL, proving that the stationary reward minimizes total variation distance.

2. The proof is clear, well motivated and easy to follow.

3. The experiment studies an important aspect of IL, which is the relationship between the amount of expert data and the IL's performance.

Weakness:
1. Similar to previous works, the practical algorithm uses soft reward (1 - min L_2 distance) to avoid reward sparsity. While a very reasonable choice, the soft reward violates the critical assumption of deterministic expert. Could the authors explain the implications of the soft reward on the theoretical results?

2. The empirical evaluation is limited, with several related questions unaddressed. For instance, the SAC expert appears to be rather weak (in terms of its performance) across different environments. It is unclear whether similar patterns would be observed if the expert matches the level of the performance observed in previous works (e.g. https://github.com/berkeleydeeprlcourse/homework/tree/master/hw1/experts). Another question is how the expert is trained has any impact on the IL performance. For instance, SAC implementation typically directly constraint the range of the action, while a Gaussian expert only clips the action as a post-processing step. In my experiences, these details have quite significant impacts on IL's performance and I hope the authors could expand on the experiments to further validate their theoretical results.

Questions:
1. Comparison between Wang et al. 2019 vs the 1-min L_2 distance reward. Could the authors motivate why the latter reward is used and how it compares to Wang et al. 2019?

2. Could the authors further elaborate on the assumption of the deterministic expert? E.g. How much does it matter in the practical algorithm? Is it even possible to differentiate between stochastic vs deterministic one from limited training data?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper shows that, for deterministic experts, imitation learning (IL) could be reduced to RL with a stationary reward. The paper thus provides theoretical grounding for several existing works. In addition, the paper also draws connection with f-divergence minimization commonly found in adversarial IL. On the other hand, the empirical results are consistent with those from existing works, but are limited in its scope. Despite the flaws, I think the work is still interesting to the community. It would be nice if the paper could include more experiments to further support the theoretical analysis.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Overview

This work presents a theoretical framework demonstrating that imitation learning (IL) with deterministic experts can be reformulated as a reinforcement learning problem using a stationary reward function. The authors establish that this stationary reward is equivalent to minimizing total variation distance between expert and learner behaviors, thereby connecting to f-divergence minimization objectives found in adversarial IL approaches. The theoretical contributions are supported by empirical validation.

## Strengths

1. The reduction of IL to RL under the deterministic expert assumption is a valuable theoretical contribution that offers justification for several existing IL methods. Particularly noteworthy is the establishment of a formal link between the proposed stationary reward formulation and f-divergence minimization, specifically demonstrating that total variation distance minimization is achieved through this approach.

2. The mathematical proofs are presented with clarity and logical coherence, making the theoretical development accessible and straightforward to follow.

3. The experimental component addresses a practically relevant question regarding how the quantity of expert demonstrations influences IL performance, which is an important consideration for practitioners.

## Weaknesses

1. In practice, the algorithm employs a soft reward formulation (defined as 1 - minimum L_2 distance) to mitigate reward sparsity issues. Although this is a pragmatic design choice, it fundamentally deviates from the deterministic expert assumption that underpins the theoretical analysis. The authors should provide discussion on how this practical modification affects the validity and applicability of the theoretical guarantees.

2. The empirical evaluation lacks sufficient breadth and leaves several important questions unresolved. Notably, the SAC-based expert demonstrates relatively weak performance across the tested environments, raising questions about whether the observed patterns would generalize to stronger expert policies comparable to those reported in prior work (e.g., https://github.com/berkeleydeeprlcourse/homework/tree/master/hw1/experts). Additionally, the influence of expert training methodology on IL outcomes deserves investigation—for instance, differences in action constraint implementation between SAC (which directly constrains action ranges) and Gaussian experts (which apply clipping post-hoc) can substantially affect results. The authors should expand their experimental scope to more thoroughly validate the theoretical findings across diverse expert configurations.

## Questions for Authors

1. How does the adopted 1 - minimum L_2 distance reward compare to the approach presented in Wang et al. 2019? What is the motivation for selecting this particular reward specification, and how do the empirical results differ between these choices?

2. The deterministic expert assumption warrants further examination. Specifically: (a) how critical is this assumption to the practical performance of the algorithm, and (b) given only finite training data, is it feasible to reliably distinguish between genuinely deterministic and stochastic expert behaviors?

## Overall Assessment

The paper makes a solid theoretical contribution by establishing that IL for deterministic experts admits a reduction to stationary reward RL, thereby providing theoretical justification for existing methods and drawing meaningful connections to adversarial IL objectives. The proofs are well-presented and the experimental approach addresses a relevant practical question. However, the empirical validation is constrained in scope, and the gap between theoretical assumptions (deterministic experts) and practical implementation (soft rewards) deserves more thorough treatment. While these limitations are notable, the theoretical insights remain valuable for the community. The work would benefit substantially from more comprehensive experiments that test the robustness of the theoretical predictions under varied expert quality levels and training procedures.

## Recommendation

**6: marginally above the acceptance threshold**

The theoretical contributions are sufficiently interesting and the presentation is clear enough to warrant acceptance, though addressing the empirical limitations would strengthen the work considerably.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
In this paper, the authors show that, for deterministic experts, imitation learning can be done by reduction
to reinforcement learning with a stationary reward. theoretical analysis both
certifies the recovery of expert reward and bounds the total variation distance
between the expert and the imitation learner, showing a link to adversarial imitation
learning. Experiments are given to confirm that the reduction works well in
practice for continuous control tasks.
reviews.main_review:
The paper includes potentially interesting results. Some comments that can improve the paper are given below. 
1) In this paper, average rewards are considered. Is there any reason to consider the average reward MDP instead of discounted MDP, which is more widely used?
2) It is not clear what the norm || ||_{TV} means.
3) It would be better to discuss what kinds of RLs can be used in Proposition 1. It is not clear in the current paper. 
4) It would be better if the definition of intrinsic reward and extrinsic reward is explained in this paper. 
5) It is not clear how the imitation learner can learn the expert policy from the intrinsic reward in section 3. 
It would be better if the process is explained in more details.
6) In the experiment, it is not clear how the comaprative analysis is done. Because the authors did not develop new algorithms, 
it would be better to explain details of the comparative analysis. 
 7) Overall, the organization of the paper can be improved further to more clearly deliver the ideas by adding more detailed backgrounds.

In sum, the paper seems to contain potentially interesting results. 
The overall organization and presentation can be further improved to improve the readability of the paper.

reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary of the Paper

This work demonstrates that imitation learning for deterministic experts can be reformulated as a reinforcement learning problem with a time-invariant reward function. The authors provide theoretical guarantees establishing both the recovery of the expert's reward structure and bounds on the total variation distance separating the expert policy from the learned imitator policy, thereby establishing connections to adversarial imitation learning. Empirical validation on continuous control benchmarks supports the feasibility of this reduction approach.

## Main Review

The paper presents contributions that warrant consideration for publication. However, several areas require clarification and improvement:

1) The reliance on average reward formulations throughout the analysis requires justification. Given that discounted reward MDPs represent the more standard and prevalent framework in the literature, why was the average reward setting chosen for this work? A clear explanation would strengthen the motivation.

2) The notation || ||_{TV} is introduced without definition. The paper would benefit from explicitly stating what this norm represents before its use in the theoretical results.

3) Proposition 1 lacks sufficient discussion regarding which classes of reinforcement learning algorithms are compatible with the proposed reduction. The current presentation does not make explicit which RL methods can be successfully applied within this framework.

4) The concepts of intrinsic and extrinsic rewards appear in the paper without proper introduction. Providing clear definitions of these terms would enhance accessibility and precision.

5) Section 3's explanation of how the imitation learner recovers the expert policy using the intrinsic reward signal remains unclear. A more thorough exposition of this mechanism, including step-by-step details, would significantly improve comprehension.

6) The experimental evaluation lacks transparency regarding the comparative methodology. Since the authors do not introduce novel algorithmic contributions, the comparative analysis should be described in substantially greater detail to establish the validity of the empirical claims.

7) The paper's overall structure could be reorganized to more effectively communicate the central ideas. Incorporating more comprehensive background material and foundational context would enhance clarity and readability.

## Summary of the Review

While the paper contains potentially valuable theoretical and empirical contributions, the presentation and exposition require substantial refinement. Improvements in organization, clarity of definitions, and depth of explanation would significantly enhance the paper's impact and accessibility.

## Rating

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper considers imitation learning problem which aims at obtaining a policy that imitate expert behavior. Authors considers using reinforcement learning with a stationary reward constructed by expert datasets. Through theoretical analysis, the corresponding imitation policy is proved to achieve high expected per-step intrinsic reward and extrinsic reward. The difference between the expert policy and the imitation policy is bounded with a high probability. Some empirical experiments are performed on continuous control tasks. The results show this method is comparable with other algorithms while the algorithm is simpler.
reviews.main_review:
The idea of using reinforcement learning to imitate expert behavior is simple to execute compared to other imitation learning methods. I have a concern regarding the sparse reward function used in this paper. When training a reinforcement learning model, the sparse reward becomes problematic when you apply RL algorithm to solve it. Although the better intrinsic reward leads to better extrinsic reward, it is still troublesome to get a good policy by any reinforcement learning algorithm with this sparse reward function. This makes the result less useful than just applying other comparison methods. Could you give more explanation on this?
In term of computation time, the sparse reward can cause problem of bad convergence rate. It is much clearer if you can provide comparison of computation time of different algorithms in the experiment section. 
Overall, this paper is well written and very clear to readers. The result is promising and interesting from a theoretical point of view.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper provides rigorous analysis for using reinforcement learning in imitation learning. The result is novel and shows the potential to accomplish imitation learning in a straightforward way. The intrinsic reward function reduced by the expert dataset is simple to construct. By setting a constant one reward for matching the demonstrated action in a demonstrated state, any reinforcement learning algorithm can provide policy for such a MDP problem. The result in this paper gives intuition of how this method performs in terms of expected return. And sufficient experimental results are shown to verify their theoretical results.

reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary of the Paper

This work addresses the imitation learning problem through a reinforcement learning framework that leverages a fixed reward function derived from expert demonstrations. The authors provide theoretical guarantees demonstrating that the resulting policy achieves strong performance in both intrinsic and extrinsic reward metrics. They establish probabilistic bounds on the divergence between the learned policy and the expert policy, and validate their approach empirically on continuous control benchmarks. The method demonstrates competitive performance relative to existing approaches while maintaining computational simplicity.

## Main Review

The proposed approach to imitation learning via reinforcement learning offers notable simplicity compared to alternative imitation learning frameworks. However, I have significant reservations about the practical implications of employing a sparse reward signal in this context. While the theoretical analysis suggests that higher intrinsic reward correlates with improved extrinsic reward, the practical challenge remains that standard reinforcement learning algorithms struggle considerably with sparse reward formulations. This fundamental difficulty raises questions about whether the empirical benefits truly outweigh those of established baseline methods. I would appreciate more thorough discussion addressing this practical limitation.

A related concern pertains to computational efficiency. The sparse reward structure may negatively impact convergence speed, a critical practical consideration. The experimental section would benefit substantially from explicit wall-clock time comparisons across the different algorithms tested, which would clarify the actual computational trade-offs involved.

On the positive side, the paper is clearly written and well-organized, making it accessible to readers. The theoretical contributions are interesting and the empirical validation provides reasonable support for the main claims.

## Summary of Review Assessment

This paper makes a solid contribution to understanding imitation learning through the lens of reinforcement learning. The approach is conceptually straightforward—constructing a reward function that assigns constant positive reward for matching expert actions in demonstrated states—allowing any RL algorithm to address the resulting MDP. The theoretical analysis provides valuable insights into the method's performance guarantees regarding expected returns. The experimental evaluation adequately supports the theoretical findings.

## Rating

**6: marginally above the acceptance threshold**

Although the better intrinsic reward leads to better extrinsic reward, it is still troublesome to get a good policy by any reinforcement learning algorithm with this sparse reward function. This makes the result less useful than just applying other comparison methods. Could you give more explanation on this?
In term of computation time, the sparse reward can cause problem of bad convergence rate. It is much clearer if you can provide comparison of computation time of different algorithms in the experiment section.
