title: ZeroFL: Efficient On-Device Training for  Federated Learning with Local Sparsity

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper studies the problem of adding sparsity to local training in federated learning. Under the approach each node uses only the top-K weights for computations in the forward and backward pass of training (and only top-K activations for computations in the backward pass). The authors empirically show that adding sparsity in this way, while speeding up local training, significantly degrades the accuracy when directly used in federated learning. To mitigate this issue the authors propose to combine sparse training with sparse aggregation since their empirical study suggests that the accuracy drop may be due to dilution of weights that are active in only a few nodes. Experiments on CIFAR10 with non-IID splits using ResNet-18 as the model show some gain in accuracy along with a drop in communication cost when using the proposed schemes.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:

1. The authors introduce a new problem of adding sparse local training to federated learning. The problem appears well motivated (sparse training can lead to significant power savings at the edge) and challenging (directly adding sparse training to conventional aggregation methods like FedAvg leads to significant accuracy drops).

2. The proposed solutions are good initial approaches backed by the empirical study and do appear to lead to some improvement in accuracy over the naive approach.

Weaknesses:

1. For a paper that relies entirely on empirical evidence for its claims, the empirical study appears a bit limited as it considers only a single dataset (CIFAR10) and a single model architecture (ResNet18). Without seeing results for some other image/text datasets and other model architectures (MLP/LSTM) it is difficult to completely believe the claims especially since the proposed solutions rely heavily on the claims that the positions of the top-K weights do not vary significantly across models and across rounds.

2. The experimental results are also a bit limited. In particular:

a) It is misleading to highlight both max accuracy and max comms savings values since the two correspond to different settings and thus cannot be achieved simultaneously. I would suggest removing the bold font for the max comms savings model and just reporting the comms savings for the different mask ratios. An interesting future direction would be to think of a metric that can combine the two since it is currently unclear if the communication saving (and local computation cost reduction) is significant enough to compensate for the drop in accuracy w.r.t conventional FL (black line in Fig. 1)

b) Since the authors acknowledge that accuracy is expected to first increase and then decrease on increasing mask ratio, it would be worth including results for all mask ratios in the appendix at least to see the trend clearly.

c) The results with i.i.d data partitioning are not presented. Since from Fig 1 it seems like accuracy drops even for the i.i.d case, it is important to show if the proposed schemes alleviate the issue in that case as well.

d) Lastly, following point 1, in my opinion all experiments should be performed with some other datasets and model architectures before the paper can be considered for acceptance.

3. (Minor) There doesn't seem to be any real difference between Top-K-Weights and the Diff on Top-K-weights in that the weight updates will be exactly the same in both cases as far as I can tell. If that is so then there are essentially 2 (and not 3) new schemes being proposed.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
The authors introduce an interesting and important problem but given that the paper relies purely on empirical evidence for its claims the current evaluation is quite limited and not enough for acceptance. I find the claims fairly intuitive, however, and so would be willing to increase my score if more empirical evidence (in line with my comments above) is presented to back them.

Comments after rebutal : I appreciate the added experiments which certainly demonstrate that the proposed schemes are able to improve performance across different datasets. I have increased my score to reflect the same.
reviews.correctness: 2: Several of the paper’s claims are incorrect or not well-supported.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper studies the problem of adding sparsity to local training in federated learning. Under the approach each node uses only the top-K weights for computations in the forward and backward pass of training (and only top-K activations for computations in the backward pass). The authors empirically show that adding sparsity in this way, while speeding up local training, significantly degrades the accuracy when directly used in federated learning. To mitigate this issue the authors propose to combine sparse training with sparse aggregation since their empirical study suggests that the accuracy drop may be due to dilution of weights that are active in only a few nodes. Experiments on CIFAR10 with non-IID splits using ResNet-18 as the model show some gain in accuracy along with a drop in communication cost when using the proposed schemes.

Main Review:
Strengths:

1. The authors introduce a new problem of adding sparse local training to federated learning. The problem appears well motivated (sparse training can lead to significant power savings at the edge) and challenging (directly adding sparse training to conventional aggregation methods like FedAvg leads to significant accuracy drops).

2. The proposed solutions are good initial approaches backed by the empirical study and do appear to lead to some improvement in accuracy over the naive approach.

Weaknesses:

1. For a paper that relies entirely on empirical evidence for its claims, the empirical study appears a bit limited as it considers only a single dataset (CIFAR10) and a single model architecture (ResNet18). Without seeing results for some other image/text datasets and other model architectures (MLP/LSTM) it is difficult to completely believe the claims especially since the proposed solutions rely heavily on the claims that the positions of the top-K weights do not vary significantly across models and across rounds.

2. The experimental results are also a bit limited. In particular:

a) It is misleading to highlight both max accuracy and max comms savings values since the two correspond to different settings and thus cannot be achieved simultaneously. I would suggest removing the bold font for the max comms savings model and just reporting the comms savings for the different mask ratios. An interesting future direction would be to think of a metric that can combine the two since it is currently unclear if the communication saving (and local computation cost reduction) is significant enough to compensate for the drop in accuracy w.r.t conventional FL (black line in Fig. 1)

b) Since the authors acknowledge that accuracy is expected to first increase and then decrease on increasing mask ratio, it would be worth including results for all mask ratios in the appendix at least to see the trend clearly.

c) The results with i.i.d data partitioning are not presented. Since from Fig 1 it seems like accuracy drops even for the i.i.d case, it is important to show if the proposed schemes alleviate the issue in that case as well.

d) Lastly, following point 1, in my opinion all experiments should be performed with some other datasets and model architectures before the paper can be considered for acceptance.

3. (Minor) There doesn't seem to be any real difference between Top-K-Weights and the Diff on Top-K-weights in that the weight updates will be exactly the same in both cases as far as I can tell. If that is so then there are essentially 2 (and not 3) new schemes being proposed.

Summary Of The Review:
The authors introduce an interesting and important problem but given that the paper relies purely on empirical evidence for its claims the current evaluation is quite limited and not enough for acceptance. I find the claims fairly intuitive, however, and so would be willing to increase my score if more empirical evidence (in line with my comments above) is presented to back them.

Comments after rebutal : I appreciate the added experiments which certainly demonstrate that the proposed schemes are able to improve performance across different datasets. I have increased my score to reflect the same.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Paper Summary

This work addresses the incorporation of sparsity into local training procedures within federated learning systems. The proposed approach restricts each participating node to utilizing only the top-K weights during both forward and backward propagation phases (and top-K activations exclusively in backward passes). While the authors demonstrate that such sparsity accelerates local computation, they also identify a substantial accuracy degradation when naively combined with standard federated aggregation methods. To address this limitation, they propose coupling sparse local training with sparse aggregation strategies, motivated by empirical observations suggesting that accuracy loss stems from the dilution of weights that maintain activity across only a subset of nodes. Evaluation on CIFAR10 with non-IID data distributions using ResNet-18 demonstrates modest accuracy improvements alongside reduced communication requirements under the proposed framework.

## Main Review

### Strengths

1. The paper identifies and formalizes a timely and practical problem—incorporating sparse local training into federated learning systems. The motivation is compelling (edge devices can achieve substantial power consumption reductions through sparsity), and the technical challenge is non-trivial (naive application of sparsity to conventional aggregation schemes like FedAvg produces pronounced accuracy degradation).

2. The proposed solutions represent reasonable initial approaches grounded in empirical analysis and demonstrate measurable accuracy improvements relative to the straightforward baseline approach.

### Weaknesses

1. Given that the paper's arguments rest entirely on empirical validation, the experimental scope appears insufficient. The evaluation covers only a single dataset (CIFAR10) and one model variant (ResNet18). The generalizability of the findings—particularly the core claim that top-K weight positions remain stable across different models and training rounds—remains unverified without additional results from diverse datasets (both vision and language-based) and alternative architectures (such as MLPs or LSTMs).

2. The experimental evaluation presents several limitations that warrant attention:

   a) The presentation conflates two distinct optimization objectives by highlighting both maximum accuracy and maximum communication savings values simultaneously, despite these occurring under different hyperparameter configurations and thus being mutually unattainable. I recommend removing emphasis from the maximum communication savings results and instead presenting communication costs across the range of mask ratios explored. A valuable future contribution would involve developing a unified metric that appropriately trades off accuracy preservation against computational and communication savings, as it remains unclear whether the observed efficiency gains adequately compensate for the accuracy loss relative to standard FL (baseline shown in Fig. 1).

   b) The authors acknowledge that accuracy exhibits an inverted-U relationship with mask ratio, yet results for intermediate mask ratios are absent from the main presentation. Reporting the complete trend across all mask ratios—at minimum in supplementary materials—would substantiate this expected behavior.

   c) Results under IID data partitioning are not provided. Since Fig. 1 suggests accuracy degradation even in the IID regime, demonstrating whether the proposed methods effectively mitigate this issue across both data distributions is essential.

   d) Building on concern 1 above, comprehensive experiments across multiple datasets and model architectures constitute a prerequisite for acceptance. The current evaluation is insufficient to validate the broad applicability of the proposed approach.

3. (Minor) The distinction between Top-K-Weights and Diff on Top-K-Weights appears unclear, as weight updates should be identical under both schemes based on the description provided. This suggests the paper proposes two substantively different approaches rather than three, which should be clarified.

## Summary

The authors present an interesting and practically motivated research problem; however, the empirical validation supporting the core claims is limited in scope and insufficient to warrant acceptance in its current form. While the underlying intuitions are reasonable, substantially expanded experimental evidence—addressing the specific concerns outlined above—is necessary to establish the robustness and generalizability of the proposed schemes. The authors' willingness to conduct additional experiments would significantly strengthen the contribution.

## Comments Following Rebuttal

The supplementary experiments provided demonstrate convincing evidence that the proposed methods achieve performance improvements across multiple datasets, substantially addressing the initial evaluation concerns. This expanded empirical validation merits an upward revision of the assessment.

## Rating

**6: marginally above the acceptance threshold**

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper investigates sparse training to accelerate on-device federated learning. It begins by taking an off-the-shelf sparse training method (SWAT) and analyzes its limitations. Then, the method proposes three strategies to improve upon SWAT. Experiments are conducted on CIFAR-10 dataset and show some improvements compared to SWAT.
reviews.main_review:
The paper has following strengths:
1.	Indeed, for more realistic deployment and to move federated learning towards practical systems, it is important to accelerate the on-device training (and not just the inference which is mainly the current practice). So, the problem the authors have considered is important.
2.	The proposed strategies clearly indicate the improvements over existing centralized sparse training technique like SWAT.
3.	Some of the insights shared (like the non-sparse weights staying the same throughout FL) were interesting.

The paper has following weaknesses:
1.	The main weakness of this paper is the experiments section. The results are presented only on CIFAR-10 dataset and do not consider many other datasets from Federated learning benchmarks (e.g., LEAF https://leaf.cmu.edu/). The authors should see relevant works like (FedProx https://arxiv.org/abs/1812.06127) and (FedMAX, https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.03657 (ECML 2020)) for details on different datasets and model types. If the experimental evaluation was comprehensive enough, this would be a very good paper (given the interesting and important problem it addresses).
2.	One other thing (although this is not the main focus of this paper), the authors should provide comparisons between strategies that result in fast convergence (without sparsity) vs. sparse methods? For example, do non-sparse, fast convergence methods (like FedProx, FedMAX, and others) result in small enough number of epochs compared to sparse methods? Can the fast convergence methods be augmented with sparisity ideas successfully without resulting in significant loss of accuracy? Some discussion and possibly performance numbers are needed here.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
Even though the reviewer is quite happy about the problem addressed in this paper, it lacks a solid experimental section. I would be willing to increase the score if the authors can present a more comprehensive results section.

Post rebuttal:
I have read the author response and other reviews. Thanks to the authors for a detailed rebuttal and new experiments. It addressed my concerns and so I have increased the rating from 5 to 6.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review (Rewritten)

## Paper Summary

This work examines the acceleration of federated learning training on edge devices through sparse training techniques. The authors begin by critiquing an existing sparse training approach (SWAT) and identifying its shortcomings. Subsequently, they introduce three novel strategies to address these limitations. The evaluation is performed on the CIFAR-10 dataset, demonstrating performance gains relative to the baseline SWAT method.

## Strengths

1. The focus on accelerating training computations at the device level (rather than just inference optimization, which dominates current practice) represents a valuable and timely research direction for practical federated learning deployment.

2. The proposed improvements demonstrate clear performance advantages when compared to existing centralized sparse training approaches such as SWAT, with measurable gains across the evaluated metrics.

3. Several of the technical observations presented in the work are noteworthy—particularly the finding that non-sparse weight parameters remain invariant throughout the federated learning process—offering useful insights into the behavior of sparse training in distributed settings.

## Weaknesses

1. The experimental evaluation constitutes the primary limitation of this submission. The authors restrict their validation to the CIFAR-10 dataset alone, neglecting to incorporate evaluation on established federated learning benchmarks available in the literature (such as LEAF: https://leaf.cmu.edu/). To strengthen the contribution substantially, the authors should consult relevant prior work including FedProx (https://arxiv.org/abs/1812.06127) and FedMAX (https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.03657, ECML 2020) to understand the diversity of datasets and model architectures typically employed in federated learning research. A more thorough experimental assessment would elevate this work considerably, given the practical significance and novelty of the problem being addressed.

2. Additionally, though not the central focus of the paper, the authors should provide empirical comparisons between sparsity-based methods and alternative acceleration techniques that achieve fast convergence without relying on sparsity. Specifically, it would be valuable to understand whether established fast convergence methods (such as FedProx and FedMAX) achieve sufficiently low epoch counts to compete with sparse approaches, and conversely, whether these fast convergence methods can be effectively combined with sparsity mechanisms while maintaining acceptable accuracy levels. The authors should include discussion and supporting experimental evidence addressing these questions.

## Summary

While the reviewer recognizes the significance and timeliness of the problem addressed in this manuscript, the experimental validation is insufficiently comprehensive. The work would merit a higher rating contingent upon the authors providing more extensive experimental results across multiple datasets and model configurations.

## Post-Rebuttal Assessment

Having reviewed the authors' response and considered feedback from other reviewers, I acknowledge the value of the detailed rebuttal and the supplementary experiments provided. These additions effectively address the primary concerns raised in the initial review, and I am therefore upgrading my assessment accordingly.

## Final Rating

**6: marginally above the acceptance threshold**

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This work proposes ZeroFL; a method that allows for sparse neural network training at the edge along with reduced upload communication, both of which being important aspects in federated learning.  ZeroFL essentially follows a prior work on sparse neural network training, namely SWAT, and adapts it appropriately for the federated setting. The SWAT method works by setting a target weight sparsity percentage (denoted by $sp$) which is enforced in both the forward and backward pass by a top-k operation on the weights. This allows for using sparse convolutions and thus offering training time speed-ups. The authors note that it is important to use the same set of sparse weights for inference as well, in order to not affect performance. 

ZeroFL then applies this idea in the federated setting by letting each client perform SWAT type of training with a target sparsity of $sp$. Instead of the clients then communicating to the server the exact set of sparse weights obtained at the end of their local training procedure, ZeroFL proposes to add an extra “slack” $r_{mask}$ and communicate $1 - sp + r_{mask}$ percentage of weights (instead of $1 - sp$). The authors motivate this change via the results of an ablation study which showed significantly lower performance when directly applying SWAT to the federated setting. They argue that with the extra $r_{mask}$ weights, a “cleaner” training signal from the clients can be given as these weights are not “corrupted” by the weights of the other clients when averaging at the server. The authors further propose three separate weights of updating the server model, i.e., 1) using the client top-k weights, 2) using the difference between the server and client top-k weights and 3) using the top-k differences between the server and client weights.  

The authors experimentally validate the ZeroFL method on cifar 10 using 100 clients and both iid and non-iid splits.
reviews.main_review:
This submission is reasonably well-written and easy to follow. The idea is simple to implement and seems to work, at least in this specific setting that the authors considered. This constitutes a nice advantage to ZeroFL. Nevertheless, I believe this submission needs quite a bit of work before I can recommend for acceptance:

- There are comparisons with other relevant baselines missing; e.g., federated dropout (which can be done on the weight level as well) is missing.
- The overall contribution seems incremental compared to the prior work of SWAT. Furthermore, the authors discuss how SWAT also employs activation sparsification but they seem to not use it at all in their experiments. 
- The overall state of the experimental evaluation is a bit weak; there is only one dataset considered where the number of clients is relatively small (100). What happens on datasets with more users for example on EMNIST / FEMNIST? Furthermore, it seems that the authors show results from single seeds and therefore it is hard to see what is the stability of their results.
- The authors don’t talk about the extra cost of transmitting the indices of the non-zero values in the main text (although they do mention the CSC format in the table caption), which can be an important additional communication cost in the case of unstructured pruning. Furthermore, at section 5 at the end, the authors mention $0.5(r_{mask }- sp)$ savings and it is not clear to me how is that derived and whether it does take into account sending the indices of the non-zero values as well.

As for other general feedback:
- At the first bullet before the related work, the authors argue that with the way their sparsity is realised  the privacy is better preserved. Could they perhaps elaborate more? To me it seems that this can actually be worse for privacy as each user can have its own sparsity pattern and thus reveal additional information compared to a server imposed sparsity pattern which is the same for all users.
- This work seems to be geared towards upload compression, although to me it seems that download compression is also feasible. Have the authors experimented with such an idea? 
- The algorithm needs some improvements, e.g., add an if statement for the different choices, as it can be confusing as it is now. Furthermore, the details Figure 3 are quite hard to see, so I would encourage the authors to use a different colour palette and intensity for the lines. 
- At the caption of table 1 the authors mention a 7.4x improvement over vanilla SWAT but the 7.4x seems to be on the dense model, i.e., when SWAT is not employed. Could the authors elaborate on what they compare against?
- Sometimes the authors refer to top-K and sometimes to top-K%. It would be better if they pick one for consistency.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall, while ZeroFL is simple, it does seem to be a straightforward extension of SWAT to the federated setting with a very minor modification. The experimental evaluation is rather weak (experiments on a single dataset) and there is an absence of other critical baselines (such as federated dropout). These are the primary issues that lead me to recommending rejection.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This work proposes ZeroFL; a method that allows for sparse neural network training at the edge along with reduced upload communication, both of which being important aspects in federated learning.  ZeroFL essentially follows a prior work on sparse neural network training, namely SWAT, and adapts it appropriately for the federated setting. The SWAT method works by setting a target weight sparsity percentage (denoted by $sp$) which is enforced in both the forward and backward pass by a top-k operation on the weights. This allows for using sparse convolutions and thus offering training time speed-ups. The authors note that it is important to use the same set of sparse weights for inference as well, in order to not affect performance. 

ZeroFL then applies this idea in the federated setting by letting each client perform SWAT type of training with a target sparsity of $sp$. Instead of the clients then communicating to the server the exact set of sparse weights obtained at the end of their local training procedure, ZeroFL proposes to add an extra “slack” $r_{mask}$ and communicate $1 - sp + r_{mask}$ percentage of weights (instead of $1 - sp$). The authors motivate this change via the results of an ablation study which showed significantly lower performance when directly applying SWAT to the federated setting. They argue that with the extra $r_{mask}$ weights, a “cleaner” training signal from the clients can be given as these weights are not “corrupted” by the weights of the other clients when averaging at the server. The authors further propose three separate weights of updating the server model, i.e., 1) using the client top-k weights, 2) using the difference between the server and client top-k weights and 3) using the top-k differences between the server and client weights.  

The authors experimentally validate the ZeroFL method on cifar 10 using 100 clients and both iid and non-iid splits.

Main Review:
This submission is reasonably well-written and easy to follow. The idea is simple to implement and seems to work, at least in this specific setting that the authors considered. This constitutes a nice advantage to ZeroFL. Nevertheless, I believe this submission needs quite a bit of work before I can recommend for acceptance:

- There are comparisons with other relevant baselines missing; e.g., federated dropout (which can be done on the weight level as well) is missing.
- The overall contribution seems incremental compared to the prior work of SWAT. Furthermore, the authors discuss how SWAT also employs activation sparsification but they seem to not use it at all in their experiments. 
- The overall state of the experimental evaluation is a bit weak; there is only one dataset considered where the number of clients is relatively small (100). What happens on datasets with more users for example on EMNIST / FEMNIST? Furthermore, it seems that the authors show results from single seeds and therefore it is hard to see what is the stability of their results.
- The authors don’t talk about the extra cost of transmitting the indices of the non-zero values in the main text (although they do mention the CSC format in the table caption), which can be an important additional communication cost in the case of unstructured pruning. Furthermore, at section 5 at the end, the authors mention $0.5(r_{mask }- sp)$ savings and it is not clear to me how is that derived and whether it does take into account sending the indices of the non-zero values as well.

As for other general feedback:
- At the first bullet before the related work, the authors argue that with the way their sparsity is realised  the privacy is better preserved. Could they perhaps elaborate more? To me it seems that this can actually be worse for privacy as each user can have its own sparsity pattern and thus reveal additional information compared to a server imposed sparsity pattern which is the same for all users.
- This work seems to be geared towards upload compression, although to me it seems that download compression is also feasible. Have the authors experimented with such an idea? 
- The algorithm needs some improvements, e.g., add an if statement for the different choices, as it can be confusing as it is now. Furthermore, the details Figure 3 are quite hard to see, so I would encourage the authors to use a different colour palette and intensity for the lines. 
- At the caption of table 1 the authors mention a 7.4x improvement over vanilla SWAT but the 7.4x seems to be on the dense model, i.e., when SWAT is not employed. Could the authors elaborate on what they compare against?
- Sometimes the authors refer to top-K and sometimes to top-K%. It would be better if they pick one for consistency.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, while ZeroFL is simple, it does seem to be a straightforward extension of SWAT to the federated setting with a very minor modification. The experimental evaluation is rather weak (experiments on a single dataset) and there is an absence of other critical baselines (such as federated dropout). These are the primary issues that lead me to recommending rejection.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: ZeroFL

## Summary

The paper introduces ZeroFL, a federated learning approach that enables sparse neural network training on edge devices while simultaneously reducing communication overhead during uploads. Building upon the SWAT methodology for sparse training, ZeroFL adapts this framework to the federated context. The core mechanism involves clients performing sparse weight training with a designated sparsity level (sp), then communicating an expanded set of weights that includes a slack term (r_mask) to transmit 1 - sp + r_mask percentage of parameters rather than just 1 - sp. The authors justify this modification through ablation studies demonstrating performance degradation when directly porting SWAT to federated settings, attributing improvements to cleaner training signals from non-corrupted weights. Three distinct server aggregation strategies are proposed: employing client top-k weights, differences between server and client top-k weights, or top-k differences. Experimental validation occurs on CIFAR-10 with 100 clients under both IID and non-IID data distributions.

## Main Strengths and Weaknesses

The paper demonstrates clear presentation and straightforward implementation, which represents a practical advantage. However, several substantial concerns limit its contribution:

**Missing Baseline Comparisons**: The evaluation lacks critical baseline methods, particularly federated dropout applied at the weight level, which would provide essential context for assessing the proposed method's relative merit.

**Limited Novelty**: The contribution appears primarily incremental relative to SWAT. Notably, while the authors acknowledge that SWAT incorporates activation sparsification, this component is entirely absent from their experimental setup, raising questions about whether the full potential of the underlying method is being explored.

**Insufficient Experimental Scope**: The evaluation is constrained to a single dataset with a modest number of clients (100). The generalizability remains unclear—what performance characteristics emerge with larger-scale deployments like EMNIST or FEMNIST? Additionally, results appear to derive from individual random seeds without variance estimates, making it difficult to assess result stability and statistical significance.

**Incomplete Communication Cost Analysis**: The paper inadequately addresses the overhead of transmitting indices for non-zero values in unstructured sparsity schemes, which constitutes a meaningful communication burden. While CSC format receives passing mention in a table caption, this critical cost is not thoroughly discussed in the main narrative. The claimed 0.5(r_mask - sp) savings reduction at the end of Section 5 lacks clear derivation, and it remains ambiguous whether index transmission costs are incorporated into this calculation.

## Additional Feedback

**Privacy Claims Require Clarification**: The initial bullet point before related work asserts improved privacy preservation through the sparsity mechanism. This assertion needs elaboration. Conversely, allowing individual clients to maintain distinct sparsity patterns could potentially compromise privacy more severely than a uniform server-imposed pattern, as heterogeneous patterns may leak additional information about local data distributions.

**Unexplored Download Compression**: While the work focuses on upload compression, download compression through sparse model distribution appears feasible. Have the authors investigated this complementary direction?

**Presentation Issues**: Algorithm presentation would benefit from explicit conditional statements distinguishing between the three aggregation choices, as the current formulation risks confusion. Figure 3 visibility is problematic; improved color schemes and line intensity would enhance readability and interpretability.

**Table 1 Caption Ambiguity**: The reported 7.4x improvement over vanilla SWAT requires clarification. The comparison appears to be against the dense model rather than against SWAT itself. This distinction should be explicitly stated to avoid misinterpretation.

**Terminology Consistency**: The paper alternates between "top-K" and "top-K%" notation. Adopting uniform terminology throughout would strengthen clarity and professionalism.

## Summary Assessment

While ZeroFL presents a simple and implementable approach, it fundamentally represents a straightforward adaptation of SWAT to federated settings with minimal novelty. The experimental evaluation suffers from limited scope (single dataset, modest client count, single-run results) and lacks essential baseline comparisons. The communication cost analysis remains incomplete, particularly regarding index transmission overhead. These limitations, combined with the incremental nature of the contribution, place the work below the acceptance threshold.

## Rating

**5: marginally below the acceptance threshold**
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This work proposes ZeroFL; a method that allows for sparse neural network training at the edge along with reduced upload communication, both of which being important aspects in federated learning.  ZeroFL essentially follows a prior work on sparse neural network training, namely SWAT, and adapts it appropriately for the federated setting. The SWAT method works by setting a target weight sparsity percentage (denoted by $sp$) which is enforced in both the forward and backward pass by a top-k operation on the weights. This allows for using sparse convolutions and thus offering training time speed-ups. The authors note that it is important to use the same set of sparse weights for inference as well, in order to not affect performance. 

ZeroFL then applies this idea in the federated setting by letting each client perform SWAT type of training with a target sparsity of $sp$. Instead of the clients then communicating to the server the exact set of sparse weights obtained at the end of their local training procedure, ZeroFL proposes to add an extra “slack” $r_{mask}$ and communicate $1 - sp + r_{mask}$ percentage of weights (instead of $1 - sp$). The authors motivate this change via the results of an ablation study which showed significantly lower performance when directly applying SWAT to the federated setting. They argue that with the extra $r_{mask}$ weights, a “cleaner” training signal from the clients can be given as these weights are not “corrupted” by the weights of the other clients when averaging at the server. The authors further propose three separate weights of updating the server model, i.e., 1) using the client top-k weights, 2) using the difference between the server and client top-k weights and 3) using the top-k differences between the server and client weights.  

The authors experimentally validate the ZeroFL method on cifar 10 using 100 clients and both iid and non-iid splits.

Main Review:
This submission is reasonably well-written and easy to follow. The idea is simple to implement and seems to work, at least in this specific setting that the authors considered. This constitutes a nice advantage to ZeroFL. Nevertheless, I believe this submission needs quite a bit of work before I can recommend for acceptance:

- There are comparisons with other relevant baselines missing; e.g., federated dropout (which can be done on the weight level as well) is missing.
- The overall contribution seems incremental compared to the prior work of SWAT. Furthermore, the authors discuss how SWAT also employs activation sparsification but they seem to not use it at all in their experiments. 
- The overall state of the experimental evaluation is a bit weak; there is only one dataset considered where the number of clients is relatively small (100). What happens on datasets with more users for example on EMNIST / FEMNIST? Furthermore, it seems that the authors show results from single seeds and therefore it is hard to see what is the stability of their results.
- The authors don’t talk about the extra cost of transmitting the indices of the non-zero values in the main text (although they do mention the CSC format in the table caption), which can be an important additional communication cost in the case of unstructured pruning. Furthermore, at section 5 at the end, the authors mention $0.5(r_{mask }- sp)$ savings and it is not clear to me how is that derived and whether it does take into account sending the indices of the non-zero values as well.

As for other general feedback:
- At the first bullet before the related work, the authors argue that with the way their sparsity is realised  the privacy is better preserved. Could they perhaps elaborate more? To me it seems that this can actually be worse for privacy as each user can have its own sparsity pattern and thus reveal additional information compared to a server imposed sparsity pattern which is the same for all users.
- This work seems to be geared towards upload compression, although to me it seems that download compression is also feasible. Have the authors experimented with such an idea? 
- The algorithm needs some improvements, e.g., add an if statement for the different choices, as it can be confusing as it is now. Furthermore, the details Figure 3 are quite hard to see, so I would encourage the authors to use a different colour palette and intensity for the lines. 
- At the caption of table 1 the authors mention a 7.4x improvement over vanilla SWAT but the 7.4x seems to be on the dense model, i.e., when SWAT is not employed. Could the authors elaborate on what they compare against?
- Sometimes the authors refer to top-K and sometimes to top-K%. It would be better if they pick one for consistency.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, while ZeroFL is simple, it does seem to be a straightforward extension of SWAT to the federated setting with a very minor modification. The experimental evaluation is rather weak (experiments on a single dataset) and there is an absence of other critical baselines (such as federated dropout). These are the primary issues that lead me to recommending rejection.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Complex Locomotion Skill Learning via Differentiable Physics

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This work proposes to learn locomotion skills on soft robots made of springs. It proposes to leverage differentiable physics along with a NN controller based on SIREN, which allows to directly learn policies by minimizing loss functions defined on trajectories. The methods allows to learn locomotion and jump behaviors on a various set of 2D and 3D mass-spring systems.
reviews.main_review:
## Strengths

* The loss function design is clear and well explained, it is specifically tailored for cyclic motion which is the main task addressed by the paper.
* The use of SIREN to process the input and output a periodical signal is quite interesting. It suits well to tasks requiring cyclic motion.
* The comparison to PPO seems fair and thorough, I appreciate that the authors put efforts into having a well-tuned baseline, something which is often overlooked.
* The control of complex mass spring system is impressive

## Weaknesses

* The claim: "To the best of our knowledge, we proposed the first differentiable physics based learning frame-
work for complex locomotion tasks using single network." is a bit fishy.
Many RL algorithms handle locomotion tasks of humanoid-like robots in simulation with a single neural network. I understand the current framework use differentiable physics for mass-spring systems, and thus is not directly applicable to classical RL benchmarks without a differentiable physics simulator. Such a claim could be made if it solved classical RL locomotion tasks + harder ones for example.

* The experiment section is not really convincing and hard to follow. 
** Table 1 is barely commented, there is only a bold number in one column. Only tanh activation function is compared to sin, while relu, gelu, sigmoid could be used for hidden layers with an activation function outputting a value in [-1, 1] for the final layer of the network. Given that using SIREN seems to be an important component, the paper would benefit from a better ablation study. 
** The importance of features section is really unclear. 
There are sentences like "The results show that each of the three parts serves as a necessary
component for a converged training. The periodic control signal serves as an important role. The
agent can hardly move without this signal" without reference to a table or a figure, so I do not know what to look to confirm the statement.
Figure 6 is also hard to read and I am not sure if the section is super insightful. To my understanding it validates the design choice of the input feature which is a bit minor compared to other components of the model.
* In the abstract, a modified Adam optimizer is mentioned "we find our adoption of batching and modified Adam optimizer effective in training complex locomotion tasks". It seems to be important according to the authors but there is no experiment on vanilla Adam vs the proposed one with reduced momentum so we cannot asses how useful it is.

## Other comments

* The abbreviations of Table 1 could be put as caption rather than footnote for readability. The back-and-forth between table and footnote is not great for the reader.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper is interesting, proposing a clean framework to perform locomotion tasks on mass-spring systems. The benefit of using differentiable physics is clearly highlighted and the use of SIREN to learn periodic motions is on point. However the experimental section is weak. For this reason I only grade it as weak-reject but would be inclined to bump it if this part is strengthened.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten Version

## Paper Summary

The authors present a framework for training locomotion behaviors on soft robotic systems composed of mass-spring networks. The approach combines differentiable physics simulation with a SIREN-based neural network controller, enabling direct policy optimization through trajectory-level loss functions. The methodology is demonstrated across various 2D and 3D mass-spring configurations, successfully learning both locomotion and jumping behaviors.

## Strengths

* The loss function formulation is transparent and methodically presented, with careful design choices that specifically accommodate the cyclic nature of the target locomotion behaviors.
* The incorporation of SIREN networks to generate periodic control outputs is a compelling design decision that naturally aligns with the periodicity requirements inherent in locomotion tasks.
* The baseline comparison with PPO is executed rigorously, with apparent attention devoted to proper hyperparameter tuning—a detail frequently neglected in comparative studies.
* The demonstrated capability to control intricate mass-spring systems represents a notable technical achievement.

## Weaknesses

* The novelty claim stating "To the best of our knowledge, we proposed the first differentiable physics based learning framework for complex locomotion tasks using single network" requires qualification. Numerous reinforcement learning approaches successfully train humanoid locomotion with single neural networks; the distinction here lies specifically in the application to mass-spring systems with differentiable physics rather than standard RL benchmarks. A more compelling claim would demonstrate advantages on both traditional locomotion benchmarks and the specialized mass-spring domain.

* The experimental validation lacks clarity and depth in several respects:
  ** Table 1 receives minimal discussion, with only isolated values highlighted. The ablation study is insufficiently comprehensive—only tanh and sine activations are compared, while other options (ReLU, GELU, sigmoid in hidden layers with constrained final outputs) warrant investigation. Given that SIREN appears central to the method, a more thorough ablation examining architectural choices would strengthen the contribution.
  ** The feature importance analysis is poorly presented and difficult to interpret. Statements asserting that "each of the three parts serves as a necessary component for converged training" and that "the periodic control signal serves an important role" lack supporting references to tables or figures, making verification impossible. Figure 6 is challenging to parse and appears to validate relatively minor design decisions regarding input features rather than substantive methodological components.

* The abstract emphasizes the significance of "batching and modified Adam optimizer" for training, suggesting this is a material contribution. However, no experimental comparison between standard Adam and the proposed variant with reduced momentum is provided, preventing assessment of its actual impact.

## Minor Issues

* Table 1 would benefit from relocating footnote content to the figure caption, reducing the need for readers to navigate between table and supplementary explanations.

## Overall Assessment

The paper presents an interesting and methodologically sound approach to learning locomotion on mass-spring systems, with clear advantages from differentiable physics and sensible use of SIREN networks for periodic motion generation. Nevertheless, the experimental evaluation substantially weakens the contribution. The ablation studies are incomplete, key design choices lack proper justification through controlled experiments, and presentation issues obscure the significance of findings. With substantial revision addressing these experimental shortcomings, this work could constitute a solid contribution.

## Rating

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper provides a practical guide on how to train an NN-based controller for locomotion tasks with robustness and efficiency. 

The tasks are categorized into motion primitives including running, jumping, and crawling, each with a manually designed objective function. Considering the periodic nature of locomotion, the author proposed to use sine activation layers and periodic signals in different phases as input. The experiments show that a larger batch size can lead to faster convergence. Compared to SGD, this paper points out that Adam is better for the tasks. The supplementary video presents that the trained networks enable real-time control of complex skills.
reviews.main_review:
Based on existing differentiable physics engines, this paper further gives empirical recipes on how to use the physics gradients to train powerful controllers, which can help the community to develop techniques related to differentiable physics. 

This method divides long-horizon tasks into smaller periods with different objectives. To avoid sparse and non-smooth rewards, objective functions are manually designed for subtasks. Periodic control input is also added to the input to actuate the controller. When training the network, the authors use batching, regularization terms, and sine activation layer to accelerate and stabilize the convergence.

When reading this paper, I have several concerns,
1. In Equation 5, how is $g_v(n)$ subtracted from $A(t)$? Do they have the same dimension? $g_v(n)$ as a velocity should have a dimension of 3 (or 6 if rotation is included). The dim of  $A(t)$ should be the DOF of actuation. Maybe you mean $||g_v(n)||$. Why only target velocity is considered, how about the height $g_h(n)$? Why “periodic control signal may induce unwanted high-frequency noises”？
2. The paper mentions "a modified Adam optimizer". I did not find how Adam is modified.
3. In Figure 4, what is the definition of one iteration? Is it one step of network update or one step of simulation?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper gives us a good example of how to train a network using differentiable physics. Their video of interactively controlling 2D and 3D robots looks interesting. Although some of the techniques like staging, batching, since activation, and periodic signals are intuitive and existing tricks, it is good to see that a combination of them can successfully train a controller.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Paper Summary

This work presents a practical framework for training neural network-based locomotion controllers that achieve both robustness and computational efficiency. The approach decomposes locomotion tasks into discrete motion primitives (such as running, jumping, and crawling), each governed by a hand-crafted objective function. Leveraging the periodic structure inherent in locomotion, the authors incorporate sine-based activation functions and phase-dependent periodic signals as controller inputs. Experimental results demonstrate that increasing batch size accelerates convergence, and the paper shows that Adam optimization outperforms SGD for these tasks. The supplementary materials showcase real-time control of sophisticated behaviors on physical systems.

## Detailed Comments

Building upon existing differentiable physics simulation frameworks, this paper contributes empirical best practices for leveraging physics-based gradients to optimize neural network controllers. This work has clear value for advancing the broader field of differentiable physics-based learning.

The core methodology segments extended tasks into shorter phases with task-specific objectives. To mitigate issues arising from sparse and ill-conditioned reward signals, the authors hand-engineer objective functions tailored to individual subtasks. Periodic control signals are incorporated into the network inputs to facilitate actuation. Training is accelerated and stabilized through several complementary techniques: batch aggregation, regularization penalties, and sine-based activation functions.

However, I have identified several substantive concerns:

1. **Mathematical Clarity Issues**: Equation 5 raises dimensional consistency questions. Specifically, how is $g_v(n)$ subtracted from $A(t)$? Given that $g_v(n)$ represents velocity (dimensionality 3, or 6 with rotational components), while $A(t)$ typically has dimensionality matching the system's degrees of freedom for actuation, these may not be compatible. Did the authors intend $||g_v(n)||$ instead? Additionally, the formulation considers only target velocity $g_v(n)$—what about height objectives $g_h(n)$? Finally, the claim that "periodic control signals may induce unwanted high-frequency noises" requires clarification and justification.

2. **Optimizer Modification Unexplained**: The paper references a "modified Adam optimizer," yet the specific modifications are not detailed anywhere in the manuscript. This requires explicit explanation.

3. **Experimental Definition Ambiguity**: Figure 4 lacks a clear definition of what constitutes "one iteration." Is this referring to a single network parameter update step, or a single simulation timestep? This distinction is critical for interpreting convergence behavior.

## Overall Assessment

This paper effectively demonstrates how differentiable physics can be harnessed to train functional controllers, providing valuable practical insights for the community. The interactive control demonstrations shown in the supplementary video are compelling. While the constituent techniques—task decomposition, batching, sine activations, and periodic signals—are individually well-established, their systematic combination yields effective results. The contribution is somewhat incremental, as these are largely known engineering practices applied in concert, but the validation across multiple locomotion tasks provides useful empirical evidence.

## Recommendation

**Rating: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)**

The paper merits publication as a solid empirical contribution that will benefit practitioners working with differentiable physics. However, the limited conceptual novelty and the unresolved technical clarifications prevent a stronger recommendation. Addressing the mathematical and methodological ambiguities noted above would strengthen the work considerably.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes to use differentiable simulators for learning locomotion skills for deformable characters. The key contribution is the experiments and analysis of a large number of design choices, including activation function, reward design, optimizer, batch size, etc. The paper finds that by combining differentiable simulator with the right design choices, the character can learn general and complex skills with one policy. The paper also shows that this combination can significantly outperform PPO.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:
1) This paper combines differentiable simulation with many carefully chosen design decisions, which enables effective learning of soft body locomotion.
2) The analysis and ablation study provides useful and practical guidance to practitioners in the field of reinforcement learning and locomotion control.

Weakness:
1) Technical contribution is lean. The Taichi differentiable simulator is an existing work. This paper seems to use it without lots of changes. The second claimed contribution "end-to-end differentiable simulation environment, including mass-spring and material point" is not well supported. The description of simulation is high level or in Section 2. It is hard to understand the scope of this contribution.

2) I hope that the paper can demonstrate more impressive and diverse results. For example, a soft character navigating uneven terrains, or a soft robot manipulating objects. A soft body character running on flat ground, as shown in this paper, has been demonstrated in several prior works, such as DiffTaichi. Even though this paper demonstrates multiple locomotion skills (turning, different speed and jumping) in one policy, the improvement over the prior work is incremental.

Additional questions and comments:
1) The paper claims in Section 2.1 that "Any physically-based deformable object simulator can be applied to our learning framework". I thought that the simulator needs to be differentiable. So only a small subset of simulation will work. Did I miss anything?

2) It is unclear to me how applicable the results of the ablation study are to other tasks. For example, the sine actuation function and the periodic signal may not be as important for manipulation tasks. If the application of this paper is only locomotion, its contributions are quite limited.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper demonstrates good results for learning soft body locomotion. Although the technical novelty is lean, I find that the analysis and ablation of various design decisions are helpful, and has practical values to guide further development in this direction. For this reason, I am slightly leaning towards accepting the paper.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes to use differentiable simulators for learning locomotion skills for deformable characters. The key contribution is the experiments and analysis of a large number of design choices, including activation function, reward design, optimizer, batch size, etc. The paper finds that by combining differentiable simulator with the right design choices, the character can learn general and complex skills with one policy. The paper also shows that this combination can significantly outperform PPO.

Main Review:
Strengths:
1) This paper combines differentiable simulation with many carefully chosen design decisions, which enables effective learning of soft body locomotion.
2) The analysis and ablation study provides useful and practical guidance to practitioners in the field of reinforcement learning and locomotion control.

Weakness:
1) Technical contribution is lean. The Taichi differentiable simulator is an existing work. This paper seems to use it without lots of changes. The second claimed contribution "end-to-end differentiable simulation environment, including mass-spring and material point" is not well supported. The description of simulation is high level or in Section 2. It is hard to understand the scope of this contribution.

2) I hope that the paper can demonstrate more impressive and diverse results. For example, a soft character navigating uneven terrains, or a soft robot manipulating objects. A soft body character running on flat ground, as shown in this paper, has been demonstrated in several prior works, such as DiffTaichi. Even though this paper demonstrates multiple locomotion skills (turning, different speed and jumping) in one policy, the improvement over the prior work is incremental.

Additional questions and comments:
1) The paper claims in Section 2.1 that "Any physically-based deformable object simulator can be applied to our learning framework". I thought that the simulator needs to be differentiable. So only a small subset of simulation will work. Did I miss anything?

2) It is unclear to me how applicable the results of the ablation study are to other tasks. For example, the sine actuation function and the periodic signal may not be as important for manipulation tasks. If the application of this paper is only locomotion, its contributions are quite limited.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper demonstrates good results for learning soft body locomotion. Although the technical novelty is lean, I find that the analysis and ablation of various design decisions are helpful, and has practical values to guide further development in this direction. For this reason, I am slightly leaning towards accepting the paper.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Paper Summary

The authors propose leveraging differentiable simulators to train locomotion controllers for deformable characters. The central contribution centers on a comprehensive empirical investigation of numerous hyperparameter and design selections—including nonlinear activation functions, reward formulation, optimization algorithms, and training batch sizes. The work demonstrates that thoughtful combinations of these design choices, integrated with differentiable simulation, enable a single policy to acquire versatile and sophisticated locomotion behaviors. The results further indicate substantial performance gains relative to standard policy gradient methods like PPO.

## Strengths

1. The paper successfully integrates differentiable simulation with a thoughtfully curated set of design decisions that collectively facilitate effective learning of deformable body locomotion.

2. The systematic analysis and comprehensive ablation studies offer valuable practical insights and actionable recommendations for practitioners working in reinforcement learning and locomotion control.

## Weaknesses

1. The technical novelty is limited. The Taichi differentiable simulator represents prior work, and the paper appears to apply it with minimal modifications. The second claimed contribution regarding "end-to-end differentiable simulation environments encompassing mass-spring and material point methods" lacks sufficient justification. The simulation methodology is presented at a high level in Section 2, making it difficult to assess the actual scope and originality of this contribution.

2. The experimental validation would benefit from more ambitious and varied demonstrations. Examples might include soft characters traversing irregular terrain or deformable robots performing object manipulation. The core result—a soft-bodied character performing locomotion on flat surfaces—has already been demonstrated in related work such as DiffTaichi. While the paper does showcase multiple locomotion variants (turning, variable speeds, jumping) within a unified policy, this represents an incremental advance over existing literature.

## Questions and Comments

1. Section 2.1 claims that "any physically-based deformable object simulator can be applied to our learning framework." However, differentiability appears to be a fundamental requirement for the approach. Is the statement overstated, or have I misunderstood the technical requirements?

2. The generalizability of the ablation study findings remains unclear. The design choices validated here—particularly sine-based actuation and periodic signals—may not transfer to fundamentally different tasks. Should the scope of applicability be restricted to locomotion, this would substantially narrow the contribution's impact.

## Overall Assessment

The paper achieves solid empirical results in soft body locomotion. While the technical novelty is modest, the systematic exploration and ablation of design decisions provides genuinely useful guidance with practical value for advancing research in this area. This practical contribution, combined with the demonstrated results, provides sufficient merit for acceptance.

**Rating: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)**

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes to learn neural network controllers in a differentiable simulator for locomotion tasks. A set of technical improvements are made and make the controllers more robust and require fewer samplers to learn. These include: using periodic activation functions, design of loss functions, and using large batch size. The paper shows learned locomotion skills of running, jumping, and crawling for different soft robots, which can be interactively controlled.
reviews.main_review:
### Strength
* +++ The paper shows great results of interactively controlling soft robot locomotion and switching among different goals.

### Weakness
* --- The overall technical contribution is limited. The proposed contribution on loss designs is limited to the locomotion tasks, and it is not clear whether these losses would generalize to other robots and the real world [1, 2].
* -- The use of the sine activation function is not well supported by the experiments. In table 1, sine function shows marginal improvement over tanh, and in table 5 of the appendix, using tanh shows much better performance over sine. Furthermore, tanh tends to become saturated during backpropagation of a long sequence. I would suggest the author also compares with ReLU.
* -- Presentation in the paper can be improved:
     1. The modified Adam optimizer is listed as one of the technical contributions. However, it is not stated clearly what is the modification and how does it compare to the original Adam optimizer. 
     2. The name of the robots are presented in Fig. 7 of Sec. 4.5 but are referenced multiple times in previous sections and table 1. It would be helpful to clarify the name of the agents early on. 
     3. Reference of Fig. 5 at the end of Sec. 4.2 seems to be incorrect, as no comparison of SGD and Adam is shown in Fig. 5.

[1] Lee, Joonho, et al. "Learning quadrupedal locomotion over challenging terrain." Science robotics 5.47 (2020).
[2] Zhao, Allan, et al. "RoboGrammar: graph grammar for terrain-optimized robot design." ACM Transactions on Graphics (TOG) 39.6 (2020): 1-16.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Paper Summary

This work presents a framework for training neural network-based controllers within a differentiable simulation environment to enable locomotion behaviors in soft robotic systems. The authors introduce several technical refinements—including the incorporation of periodic activation functions, carefully designed objective functions, and the use of large batch training—that improve controller robustness and reduce sample complexity. The resulting controllers demonstrate various locomotion skills (running, jumping, crawling) across multiple soft robot morphologies and support interactive control with goal switching capabilities.

## Strengths

* +++ The paper demonstrates compelling experimental results, particularly in the interactive control of soft robot locomotion behaviors and the ability to seamlessly transition between different locomotion objectives.

## Weaknesses

* --- The scope of technical novelty is relatively modest. The proposed loss function designs are tailored specifically to locomotion tasks, raising questions about their applicability beyond this domain and their transferability to real-world robotic systems [1, 2].

* -- The justification for using sine-based activation functions lacks sufficient experimental support. Table 1 reveals only marginal improvements of sine over tanh, while Table 5 in the appendix actually demonstrates superior performance with tanh. Additionally, tanh's tendency to saturate during backpropagation through extended sequences is a known limitation. The authors should include comparisons with ReLU-based architectures to strengthen their activation function analysis.

* -- The paper's presentation requires substantial refinement:
     1. The modified Adam optimizer is claimed as a key technical contribution, yet the specific modifications and performance differences relative to the standard Adam optimizer are not adequately explained.
     2. Robot morphology names first appear in Fig. 7 (Sec. 4.5) but are referenced repeatedly throughout earlier sections and in Table 1. These identifiers should be introduced much earlier in the manuscript for reader clarity.
     3. The citation of Fig. 5 at the conclusion of Sec. 4.2 appears to be erroneous, as this figure does not contain the promised comparison between SGD and Adam optimization algorithms.

[1] Lee, Joonho, et al. "Learning quadrupedal locomotion over challenging terrain." Science robotics 5.47 (2020).
[2] Zhao, Allan, et al. "RoboGrammar: graph grammar for terrain-optimized robot design." ACM Transactions on Graphics (TOG) 39.6 (2020): 1-16.

## Summary Assessment

While the paper presents noteworthy experimental achievements in soft robot locomotion control, the underlying technical innovations are limited in scope and generalizability. Additionally, several presentation issues detract from the work's clarity and impact. The authors should address these substantive concerns to bring the manuscript closer to acceptance standards.

## Recommendation

**5: marginally below the acceptance threshold**

The work shows promise but requires addressing the identified weaknesses—particularly regarding technical contribution scope, activation function justification, and presentation clarity—before it meets the publication bar.

title: On the Unreasonable Effectiveness of Feature Propagation in Learning on Graphs with Missing Node Features

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors consider the case of training GNNs with missing features. The proposed framework consists of a diffusion based step prior to training the GNN. A discretization of the approach leads to what they term "feature propagation", a scalable method to impute features on the graph. The assumption is that the energy function that determines how related the features are to each other can be learnt from the data. The authors consider the gradient flow and it's solution to minimize the dirichlet energy, which is far more scalable than computing the closed form solution. Experiments on multiple datasets show that the proposed method outperforms several baselines.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:

The paper is well written, and easy to follow. The problem the authors address is interesting, and the solution provided is simple, yet elegant. The experimental results are thorough, both in terms of comparing performance and speed of the method. 

Weaknesses:

It's not completely clear how the proposed method extends to vector valued features. Is the process repeated independently for each feature? or is there some "interdependencies" between feature dimensions that can be made use of? How does that affect the computational complexity?
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
Initial assessment : accept

The paper is well written, and easy to follow. The problem the authors address is interesting, and the solution provided is simple, yet elegant. The experimental results are thorough, both in terms of comparing performance and speed of the method. Missing value imputation is a problem that is often ill-addressed when it comes to ML research. In this paper, the authors specifically look at imputation in the context of training GNNs, and use the graph to perform the imputation. 

I feel the authors need to address the difference between the proposed method and label propagation a bit more. They claim it's different because of labels being discrete and the diffusion operator being different, but I'm not clear why that's a deal breaker. Won't the algorithm be the same but with a new operator? 

Another point to address is the extension to vector valued features. They claim the extension is straightforward, but a note to this effect will be nice. 

other comments:
P1 last paragraph : I’m not sure if being unaware to the task is a bad thing necessarily.  isn't it better to do imputation in a task agnostic fashion?

Fig 3: Please make the text larger. The figures are hard to read. 

Fig 3: Also any intuition why Random is so much worse than Zero in all these datasets?
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.

reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary of the Paper

This work addresses the challenge of training Graph Neural Networks when node features are incomplete. The authors propose a diffusion-based approach that serves as a preprocessing step before GNN training. Through discretization, this yields "feature propagation," a computationally efficient imputation technique. The core idea is that an energy function governing feature relationships can be learned directly from data. Rather than computing the closed-form solution, the authors leverage gradient flow to minimize the Dirichlet energy, offering significant scalability improvements. Experimental validation across multiple benchmarks demonstrates superior performance compared to existing alternatives.

## Strengths

The manuscript is clearly articulated and accessible to readers. The underlying problem is compelling, and the proposed solution strikes a good balance between simplicity and sophistication. The experimental evaluation is comprehensive, providing detailed comparisons of both predictive performance and computational efficiency across different methods.

## Weaknesses

A significant gap remains regarding how the methodology generalizes to multi-dimensional feature vectors. The paper does not adequately clarify whether feature dimensions are processed independently or whether cross-dimensional dependencies are exploited. This distinction has important implications for computational complexity that warrant explicit discussion.

The distinction from label propagation requires deeper clarification. While the authors mention that label discreteness and diffusion operator differences set their approach apart, the fundamental distinction remains somewhat opaque. If the underlying algorithm structure is essentially identical but with a different operator, the practical and conceptual advantages deserve more thorough explanation.

The claim that extending to vector-valued features is straightforward needs substantiation. A concrete discussion or even a brief technical note explaining this extension would strengthen the paper considerably.

## Additional Comments

**Page 1, final paragraph:** The characterization of task-agnostic imputation as inherently problematic warrants reconsideration. In fact, performing imputation independent of downstream task specifications could be viewed as advantageous rather than limiting.

**Figure 3:** The text size is insufficient for legibility. Please enlarge labels and annotations for improved readability.

**Figure 3:** An explanation for why the Random baseline performs substantially worse than Zero initialization across all datasets would provide valuable intuition about the method's behavior.

## Overall Assessment

**Rating: 8 (Accept, good paper)**

This is a solid contribution that tackles an understudied yet practically important problem. The proposed diffusion-based imputation framework is intuitive and well-motivated, and the experimental results convincingly demonstrate its effectiveness. With minor revisions addressing the clarifications noted above—particularly regarding vector-valued features and the relationship to label propagation—this work would be a valuable addition to the literature.
