reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Paper Summary

This work presents a Visually-Informed Dereverberation of Audio (VIDA) framework that leverages visual cues alongside audio signals to perform dereverberation. The authors develop both synthetic and real-world datasets to validate their approach and demonstrate its applicability across multiple downstream tasks including automatic speech recognition, speech enhancement, and speaker identification. The central novelty lies in incorporating visual modality as an auxiliary signal for the dereverberation task.

## Detailed Comments

A significant concern with this work is the disconnect between the claimed benefits of visual information and the empirical results presented. Examining Table 1 reveals that improvements from the audio-only dereverberation baseline to the proposed VIDA model are quite marginal. Specifically, PESQ scores improve only from 2.32 to 2.37, WER-FT decreases modestly from 3.76 to 3.66, and SID EER drops from 2.61 to 2.40. These modest gains raise fundamental questions regarding whether the visual component genuinely contributes meaningfully to the dereverberation process, or whether the audio pathway remains the dominant source of performance gains. A deeper analysis is warranted to disentangle the contributions of each modality and understand the actual utility of visual information in this context.

From a technical perspective, the novelty is somewhat limited. The primary contributions appear to be restricted to the introduction of a new dataset and the incorporation of a ResNet architecture for processing visual features. Such architectural choices are relatively standard, and comparable multimodal fusion strategies leveraging visual cues have already been explored in related audio-visual enhancement tasks, particularly those utilizing lip-reading information. The incremental nature of these technical advances limits the significance of the contribution.

## Overall Assessment

In its present form, this manuscript provides insufficient novelty and impact for acceptance. The empirical gains attributable to the visual modality are negligible, and the technical contributions lack sufficient originality relative to existing work in multimodal audio processing. The paper would benefit from more substantial improvements in performance and deeper investigation into why visual information provides such limited benefit.

## Recommendation

**3: Reject - Not suitable for publication in its current form**

title: Layer-wise Adaptive Model Aggregation for Scalable Federated Learning

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes a novel adaptive model aggregating schema under a federated learning setting, where it effective reduces the communication overhead without significantly sacrificing the generalization performance. Both empirical study and theoretical analysis are included to justify the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed method.
reviews.main_review:
Effectively reducing the communication overhead is an important problem for federated learning, with the recent advance of layer-wise model freezing training, this paper manages to extend this idea for network traffic reduction in federated learning. Concretely, I list the main strengths and minor weakness of this paper as below:

Advantages:

+ To leverage layer-wise model freezing for federated learning is a simple but novel idea. According to the empirical study, this method seems to be effective and efficient. 
+ The paper also includes tight convergence analysis for the proposed method. 

Disadvantages:
+ The communication cost calculated in Section 6 is a little sloppy, in practices, there are plenty of system advances managing to hide the communication overhead within the computation slots, e.g. PyTorch-DDP (https://arxiv.org/abs/2006.15704). In this fashion, the actual runtime may not be simply linear to the amount of network traffic. Perhaps, a more sophisticated cost model would consider both the computation power of the edge device and network condition during training, then model the cost including the mechanism of overlapping communication and computation to estimate the actual runtime.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
The idea of utilizing layer-wise model freezing for federated learning is an interesting idea, the paper includes reasonable empirical study and convergence analysis to illustrate the effectiveness of this proposed approach. 
Although some part of the cost model in the simulation is a little sloppy, I tend to advocate the acceptance of this paper.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes a novel adaptive model aggregating schema under a federated learning setting, where it effective reduces the communication overhead without significantly sacrificing the generalization performance. Both empirical study and theoretical analysis are included to justify the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed method.

Main Review:
Effectively reducing the communication overhead is an important problem for federated learning, with the recent advance of layer-wise model freezing training, this paper manages to extend this idea for network traffic reduction in federated learning. Concretely, I list the main strengths and minor weakness of this paper as below:

Advantages:

+ To leverage layer-wise model freezing for federated learning is a simple but novel idea. According to the empirical study, this method seems to be effective and efficient. 
+ The paper also includes tight convergence analysis for the proposed method. 

Disadvantages:
+ The communication cost calculated in Section 6 is a little sloppy, in practices, there are plenty of system advances managing to hide the communication overhead within the computation slots, e.g. PyTorch-DDP (https://arxiv.org/abs/2006.15704). In this fashion, the actual runtime may not be simply linear to the amount of network traffic. Perhaps, a more sophisticated cost model would consider both the computation power of the edge device and network condition during training, then model the cost including the mechanism of overlapping communication and computation to estimate the actual runtime.

Summary Of The Review:
The idea of utilizing layer-wise model freezing for federated learning is an interesting idea, the paper includes reasonable empirical study and convergence analysis to illustrate the effectiveness of this proposed approach. 
Although some part of the cost model in the simulation is a little sloppy, I tend to advocate the acceptance of this paper.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Paper Summary

This work introduces an innovative adaptive model aggregation framework designed for federated learning environments. The approach successfully mitigates communication costs while maintaining reasonable generalization performance. The paper combines empirical validation with theoretical justification to demonstrate the viability of the proposed methodology.

## Detailed Review

Addressing communication efficiency represents a critical challenge in federated learning. Building upon recent developments in layer-wise model freezing strategies, this paper extends such techniques to minimize network traffic during federated learning. Below, I outline the key strengths and areas of concern:

**Strengths:**

+ The application of layer-wise model freezing to the federated learning context is an elegant and original contribution. The empirical results demonstrate that this approach achieves both effectiveness and computational efficiency in practice.
+ The paper provides rigorous convergence analysis that establishes theoretical guarantees for the proposed method.

**Weaknesses:**

+ The communication cost modeling presented in Section 6 lacks sophistication. In real-world deployments, modern system frameworks such as PyTorch-DDP (https://arxiv.org/abs/2006.15704) employ techniques to overlap communication with computation, effectively amortizing communication overhead within computational periods. Consequently, actual runtime does not necessarily scale linearly with network traffic volume. A more comprehensive cost model would incorporate device computational capacity and network conditions as variables, and would account for communication-computation overlap mechanisms to provide more accurate runtime predictions.

## Overall Assessment

The core concept of applying layer-wise model freezing to federated learning is compelling. The paper presents a credible empirical evaluation alongside solid theoretical analysis that substantiate the method's efficacy.

While certain aspects of the communication cost analysis in the experimental section warrant refinement, the overall contribution is sufficiently strong to merit publication.

## Recommendation

**Accept – Good Paper (Rating: 8)**

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes an adaptive interval schema for layer-wise model aggregation in federated settings. The aim of the proposed method is to reduce communication costs by adaptively decreasing the frequency of layer-wise aggregation with consideration of model discrepancy.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:

1. It is an interesting idea to adjust the aggregation interval for layer-wise aggregation.
2. Detailed theoretical analysis and experiments are conducted.
3. The paper is well-written.


Weaknesses:

1. It is uncertain that the proposed method can really reduce communication costs. Due to the reduced frequency of aggregation and adopt layer-wise partial model aggregation strategy in each round, it is very likely that the proposed method will decrease the convergence speed of the global model. Therefore, the proposed method needs more communication rounds to achieve a convergence state, thus the total communication costs are still very high. Moreover, at each communication round, the client still needs to download the full model from the server. Therefore, the downloading communication costs haven't been improved. 
2. In the convergence analysis, Assumption 4 is a little tricky, and helps to avoid much detailed and further analysis. The convergence rate is O(1/m^½ K^½), which is loose compared to some convergence analysis of FedAvg, such as ON THE CONVERGENCE OF FEDAVG ON NON-IID DATA (https://arxiv.org/abs/1907.02189).
3. The experiment is insufficient to support their claims. For example, the only baseline is FedAvg, and it is necessary to choose some compression methods as a baseline. Moreover, the communication cost should consider the number of communication rounds to achieve a convergence state.


Questions and suggestions:

1. Algorithm 1 should explicitly state which steps are executed on the server or client. For example, line 3 should be on the client, and lines from 4 to 10 are on the server. Moreover, it should explicitly highlight when the communication occurs.
2. It is necessary to draw the convergence curves of the proposed methods and baselines.
3. It is unclear how to construct non-IID scenarios in the experiment setting.
4. There are some federated learning methods that only update parts of its model. This paper should also consider these baseline methods.

# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary of the Paper

The paper introduces an adaptive mechanism for controlling aggregation intervals during layer-wise model aggregation in federated learning environments. The primary objective is to minimize communication overhead by dynamically modulating the frequency at which layers are aggregated, taking into account variations in model parameters across clients.

## Main Review

### Strengths

1. The concept of dynamically adjusting aggregation intervals tailored to layer-wise aggregation represents a novel contribution to the field.
2. The work includes comprehensive theoretical analysis supported by empirical validation.
3. The manuscript is clearly presented and well-organized.

### Weaknesses

1. The claimed communication cost reduction is questionable. Since the approach employs reduced aggregation frequency combined with partial layer-wise aggregation in each round, convergence speed of the global model is likely to suffer. This slowdown would necessitate additional communication rounds to reach convergence, potentially negating any communication savings. Furthermore, clients must still retrieve the complete model from the server at each communication round, meaning download costs remain unaddressed. The overall communication budget may therefore remain substantial despite the proposed modifications.

2. The convergence analysis relies on Assumption 4, which appears somewhat restrictive and conveniently sidesteps deeper technical investigation. The derived convergence rate of O(1/m^½ K^½) is notably loose compared to established convergence guarantees for FedAvg in non-IID settings, as demonstrated in prior work (e.g., "On the Convergence of FedAvg on Non-IID Data").

3. The experimental evaluation is insufficient to substantiate the paper's central claims. Specifically, FedAvg alone serves as the baseline, whereas comparisons with model compression techniques are necessary for proper evaluation. Additionally, the communication cost assessment must account for the total number of rounds required to achieve convergence, not merely per-round costs.

### Questions and Suggestions

1. Algorithm 1 should clearly delineate which operations occur on the client side versus the server side. For instance, line 3 should be marked as client-side execution, while lines 4-10 should be identified as server-side operations. The locations where communication exchanges take place should also be explicitly marked.

2. Convergence trajectories comparing the proposed method with all baseline approaches should be presented graphically.

3. The methodology for constructing non-IID data distributions in the experimental setup requires clarification.

4. The paper should incorporate additional baseline methods from federated learning literature that similarly employ partial model updates during training. References to relevant work in this area should be consulted (e.g., Collins et al. 2021 and related recent publications on partial model updates).

## Summary of the Review

The paper addresses an interesting problem through layer-wise adaptive aggregation to mitigate communication expenses while maintaining performance parity with FedAvg. However, the methodological design, theoretical guarantees, and experimental validation collectively fall short of substantiating these claims.

## Rating

**5: marginally below the acceptance threshold**

reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary of the Paper

The authors introduce a layer-wise adaptive aggregation strategy for federated learning designed to minimize communication overhead. The core contribution involves measuring the divergence between local and global model parameters, then using this measurement to dynamically control aggregation frequency on a per-layer basis. By extending the intervals between aggregation rounds and reducing how often aggregation occurs, the approach aims to decrease communication costs while maintaining performance on both IID and non-IID datasets compared to the FedAvg baseline.

## Main Review

The paper addresses an important problem in federated learning by proposing a method to reduce communication burden through adaptive layer-wise aggregation intervals, determined by quantifying the divergence between local and global models.

However, several significant concerns arise:

1) **Limited Experimental Comparisons**: The evaluation relies exclusively on FedAvg as a baseline, which is insufficient for assessing the state of the field. The authors should include comparisons against other periodic aggregation methods such as FedProx, FedNova, and SCAFFOLD, as well as asynchronous approaches. Relevant work in this area includes:
   - Chen, Yang, Xiaoyan Sun, and Yaochu Jin. "Communication-efficient federated deep learning with layerwise asynchronous model update and temporally weighted aggregation." IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems 31.10 (2019): 4229-4238.
   - Xie, C., Koyejo, S., & Gupta, I. (2019). Asynchronous federated optimization. arXiv preprint arXiv:1903.03934.

2) **Unclear Accuracy-Cost Tradeoff**: The experiments demonstrate that FedLAMA incurs noticeable accuracy degradation. While the paper reports communication cost reduction ratios, it fails to present absolute communication time measurements or provide a clear analysis of whether the accuracy loss justifies the communication savings. This is particularly problematic for practical applications where model accuracy is often the primary concern, especially in industrial settings where total training time may already be acceptable (e.g., within a few hours).

## Summary of the Review

The authors have made a reasonable effort to develop a theoretical framework and provide experimental validation for their federated optimization approach. Nevertheless, the experimental evaluation remains insufficient to convincingly demonstrate both the effectiveness and practical viability of the proposed method.

## Rating

**5: marginally below the acceptance threshold**

reviews.text:
# Peer Review (Rewritten)

## Summary

The authors propose an adaptive aggregation framework designed for federated learning systems, with accompanying theoretical convergence guarantees that characterize the relationship between aggregation intervals and convergence behavior. Experimental validation demonstrates potential reductions in communication overhead compared to baseline approaches.

## Strengths

1. The research addresses a practically relevant problem in federated learning, and the proposed adaptive aggregation strategy represents a meaningful contribution to the field.

2. The paper includes formal convergence rate analysis, providing theoretical grounding for the approach.

3. The experimental evaluation is comprehensive in scope and demonstrates the practical applicability of the method.

## Weaknesses

1. The theoretical analysis contains several mathematical errors that undermine confidence in the results. Specifically, Equation (15) contains an error, as does Equation (25). These mistakes require careful revision and correction.

2. Lemma 5.2 is stated without sufficient conditions. The step size parameter η requires an explicit constraint or bound to ensure the lemma holds properly.

3. The derived convergence bound exhibits worse performance characteristics than the standard FedAvg baseline. Specifically, the paper claims that FedLAMA achieves linear speedup when K=O(m³) (as noted in Remark 1), whereas the related work [1] establishes linear speedup with only K=O(m). This represents a substantially weaker convergence guarantee, suggesting the theoretical contribution is limited compared to existing results.

[1] On the Linear Speedup Analysis of Communication Efficient Momentum SGD for Distributed Non-Convex Optimization

## Overall Assessment

While the paper tackles an important problem and proposes a novel solution with experimental support, the theoretical analysis suffers from notable deficiencies. The mathematical errors in key equations, missing constraints in the lemmas, and inferior convergence bounds relative to prior work collectively indicate that the theoretical contributions require substantial revision before publication.

## Recommendation

**5: marginally below the acceptance threshold**

The work shows promise in its core ideas and experimental validation, but the theoretical foundation must be strengthened significantly to meet publication standards. The authors should address all mathematical errors, properly specify all constraints, and provide a more compelling theoretical justification relative to existing methods.

title: Stability based Generalization Bounds for Exponential Family Langevin Dynamics

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper studies the generalization of iterative noisy learning algorithms. First of all, the authors provide a new generalization bound based on the Hellinger distance. This bound is basically based on the Hellinger distance between the output of the algorithm when one of the points in the training set is replaced with a fresh sample. They they use this bound to provide a generalization error guarantee for a broad class of noisy iterative algorithm which is defined as W_t = W_{t-1} - eta_t, where eta_t conditioned on the past iterated follows an exponential family distribution which for instance include SGLD. For this broad class, they provide a unified generalization bound that includes SGLD and Noisy Sign-SGD. Finally, the authors provide numerical study of their bound for several benchmarks and show that their bound achieves better estimate of generalization error.
reviews.main_review:
- Using the generalization bound in Theorem: In proposition 1, the authors provide a generalization bound based on the distributional stability in terms of Hellinger distance. However, in most of the results in the paper they mainly use the upper bound based on KL divergence (Prop. 2). Is that correct?  

- The dependence of the learning rate on the optimization trajectory: In your bound alpha_t depends on the dataset and trajectory. It basically depends on some sort of Lipschitz constant of the loss function. For instance in SGLD, alpha_t depends on the learning rate and inverse temperatures. Therefore, I think it can limit us to use very small learning rates. Also, how did you make sure that this constraint is satisfied in your numerical studies?

- The comparison with Rodríguez-Gálvez et al.: Your bound is very similar to the generalization bound in Haghifam et al'2020. As you mentioned the bound in Haghifam et al'2020 is stated for LD algorithm. Recently, the bound in Haghifam et al'2020 is extended to SGLD in [1]. The bound in Rodríguez-Gálvez et al has worse dependence on n (1/sqrt(n)), however, they have a decaying factor in their bound to compensate using of the chain rule. It would be nice to numerically compare your bound with the bound in [1]. 

- Applying your framework for analyzing SGD:  What are the roadblocks for applying your bound to analyzing SGD?

- Surrogate loss: I think you should distinguish the loss function that we use to measure the generalization error with the loss function that is used for training. You should use different notations. 


[1] "On random subset generalization error bounds and the stochastic gradient langevin dynamics algorithm", Borja Rodríguez-Gálvez, Germán Bassi, Ragnar Thobaben, Mikael Skoglund

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper studies the generalization of iterative noisy learning algorithms. First of all, the authors provide a new generalization bound based on the Hellinger distance. This bound is basically based on the Hellinger distance between the output of the algorithm when one of the points in the training set is replaced with a fresh sample. They they use this bound to provide a generalization error guarantee for a broad class of noisy iterative algorithm which is defined as W_t = W_{t-1} - eta_t, where eta_t conditioned on the past iterated follows an exponential family distribution which for instance include SGLD. For this broad class, they provide a unified generalization bound that includes SGLD and Noisy Sign-SGD. Finally, the authors provide numerical study of their bound for several benchmarks and show that their bound achieves better estimate of generalization error.

Main Review:
- Using the generalization bound in Theorem: In proposition 1, the authors provide a generalization bound based on the distributional stability in terms of Hellinger distance. However, in most of the results in the paper they mainly use the upper bound based on KL divergence (Prop. 2). Is that correct?  

- The dependence of the learning rate on the optimization trajectory: In your bound alpha_t depends on the dataset and trajectory. It basically depends on some sort of Lipschitz constant of the loss function. For instance in SGLD, alpha_t depends on the learning rate and inverse temperatures. Therefore, I think it can limit us to use very small learning rates. Also, how did you make sure that this constraint is satisfied in your numerical studies?

- The comparison with Rodríguez-Gálvez et al.: Your bound is very similar to the generalization bound in Haghifam et al'2020. As you mentioned the bound in Haghifam et al'2020 is stated for LD algorithm. Recently, the bound in Haghifam et al'2020 is extended to SGLD in [1]. The bound in Rodríguez-Gálvez et al has worse dependence on n (1/sqrt(n)), however, they have a decaying factor in their bound to compensate using of the chain rule. It would be nice to numerically compare your bound with the bound in [1]. 

- Applying your framework for analyzing SGD:  What are the roadblocks for applying your bound to analyzing SGD?

- Surrogate loss: I think you should distinguish the loss function that we use to measure the generalization error with the loss function that is used for training. You should use different notations. 


[1] "On random subset generalization error bounds and the stochastic gradient langevin dynamics algorithm", Borja Rodríguez-Gálvez, Germán Bassi, Ragnar Thobaben, Mikael Skoglund

Summary Of The Review:
Very interesting and solid contribution!

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
# Peer Review (Rewritten)

## Paper Summary

The manuscript investigates generalization properties of iterative algorithms with noise injection. The authors introduce a novel generalization bound grounded in Hellinger distance, constructed by measuring divergence between algorithm outputs when training data points are perturbed. They subsequently apply this theoretical framework to a broad family of noisy iterative methods characterized by the update rule W_t = W_{t-1} - eta_t, where eta_t follows an exponential family distribution conditioned on prior iterations—encompassing methods such as SGLD. A unified generalization guarantee is established for this algorithm class, with empirical validation on multiple datasets demonstrating improved generalization error estimates relative to existing bounds.

## Detailed Comments

- **Choice of divergence measure in theoretical results**: Proposition 1 establishes a generalization bound via distributional stability using Hellinger distance. However, the subsequent analysis predominantly relies on the KL divergence-based upper bound presented in Proposition 2. Could the authors clarify whether this represents the intended approach, and if so, explain the rationale for this transition?

- **Learning rate constraints and practical implications**: The parameter α_t in the proposed bound exhibits dependence on both the dataset and the optimization trajectory, specifically through a Lipschitz-type constant of the loss function. For SGLD specifically, this translates to constraints involving the learning rate and inverse temperature parameters. This dependency may impose restrictive requirements on learning rate selection. Additionally, it would be valuable to understand how these constraints were verified during the experimental evaluation.

- **Relationship to concurrent work**: The proposed bound shares substantial similarities with the generalization bound from Haghifam et al. (2020), which was originally developed for Langevin dynamics. Recent work by Rodríguez-Gálvez et al. [1] has extended this framework to SGLD. While the Rodríguez-Gálvez et al. bound exhibits suboptimal dependence on sample size (1/√n), it incorporates a decay factor derived from careful chain rule application. A direct numerical comparison between your bound and the Rodríguez-Gálvez et al. result would strengthen the empirical validation.

- **Extension to non-noisy optimization**: What technical obstacles prevent application of your analytical framework to standard SGD? Identifying these barriers would clarify the scope and limitations of your approach.

- **Notation for loss functions**: The presentation would benefit from distinguishing between the loss function employed for training and the loss function used to evaluate generalization error through explicit notational differences.

## Overall Assessment

This is a valuable and technically sound contribution to the generalization theory literature.

## Recommendation

**8: accept, good paper**

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper improves generalization bounds based on the notion of stability. The notion of stability measures the effect of resampling a data point to the output of the algorithm. The paper improves known analyses in two directions: 
- It proposes to use the notion of expected stability rather than uniform stability, which should to be tighter.
- It generalizes the analysis to noises in an exponential family rather than Gaussian noise.
reviews.main_review:
This paper provides several technical improvements to a recent line of work that I am not familiar with. Thus I can hardly review that relevance  and novelty of these improvements (and I am thus putting a low confidence score). I am concerned by the fact that the article is hard to read in a self-contained way. The authors largely comment the articulation of their work with similar works, but almost never comment the relevance of their work for a wider community. 

Another concern is that the article provides two improvements listed above, but I could not perceive why it was coherent to present them in a common article. This might add some confusion to the paper. 

As a naive question, I would like to ask why expected stability bounds are preferred. It seems that they involve expectations that are hard to compute analytically, at best one can approximate these expectations by sampling. How should these bounds be used in the end? In practice, to choose stopping criteria for algorithms, for instance? Or in theory, to analyze the performance of some algorithms?

I found the generalization from Gaussian noise to exponential families novel and interesting.

In Section 2, I find difficult to determine what are your contributions and what was already known. For instance, Proposition 2 seems well-known right? Why not giving some references rather than proving it? Similarly, to what extent was Proposition 1 known?

Minor comments:
-  Section 2: "D be an unknown distribution OVER Z" (not "of")
- Why do you introduce the notation W_{0:(t-1)} = w_{0:(t-1)}? I am under the impression that this is twice the same object, but I might misunderstand.
- You sometimes use the notation KL(P,Q) and KL(P||Q).
- Conflict of notation between S_0 and S_n (for n=0!).

reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten Version

## Summary

This manuscript advances generalization bounds through the lens of algorithmic stability, which quantifies how sensitive an algorithm's output is to individual data point modifications. The authors make two primary technical contributions: (1) introducing expected stability as a framework that should yield tighter bounds compared to the standard uniform stability approach, and (2) extending the theoretical analysis beyond Gaussian noise to accommodate general exponential family distributions.

## Main Comments

While this paper presents several technical refinements to a recent research direction, I must acknowledge my limited familiarity with this specific line of work, which constrains my ability to fully assess the significance and originality of these contributions (hence my conservative confidence rating). A fundamental issue is that the manuscript lacks self-contained exposition. The authors devote considerable effort to positioning their work relative to closely related papers but provide minimal discussion of why these results matter for the broader research community.

Additionally, I am uncertain about the coherence of combining two distinct improvements within a single paper. The two main contributions—the shift from uniform to expected stability, and the generalization to exponential family noise—feel somewhat disconnected, and their joint presentation may create unnecessary confusion for readers.

I also have a substantive question regarding the practical utility of expected stability bounds. Since these bounds involve expectations that are typically intractable to compute analytically, they would seem to require Monte Carlo approximation in practice. This raises an important question about their intended application: Are these bounds meant to guide algorithm design and stopping criteria in practice? Or are they primarily theoretical tools for analyzing algorithm performance? This distinction should be clarified.

The extension from Gaussian to exponential family noise distributions is a valuable contribution that I find conceptually interesting and novel.

In Section 2, the delineation between the authors' contributions and previously established results remains unclear. For example, Proposition 2 appears to be standard material—would it not be more efficient to cite existing references rather than provide a full proof? Similarly, the novelty status of Proposition 1 deserves explicit clarification.

## Minor Issues

- Section 2: "D be an unknown distribution OVER Z" (should use "over" rather than "of")
- The notation W_{0:(t-1)} = w_{0:(t-1)} is introduced but appears to represent the same object; clarification would be helpful
- Inconsistent notation for Kullback-Leibler divergence: sometimes written as KL(P,Q) and elsewhere as KL(P||Q)
- Notational conflict between S_0 and S_n when n=0

## Summary Assessment

My review confidence is limited due to insufficient background in this specialized research area. To strengthen the manuscript, I would recommend: (1) providing clearer motivation for why these specific improvements matter to a broader audience, (2) better justifying the presentation of both contributions together, and (3) offering concrete guidance on the practical application and computational feasibility of the expected stability bounds.

## Rating

**5: marginally below the acceptance threshold**

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper provides generalization guarantees for exponential family sgld which is a more general setup than standard gaussian noise langevin dynamics as it allows for noise settings from any member of the exponential family. The authors point out that the setup they consider is a strict generalization of the standard langevin dynamics, and give another explicit example of sign sgd based generalization. 

The authors use and show gradient discrepancy as opposed to gradient norms as used by Li et al, and gradient incoherence as used by Negrea et al. but the latter have 1/\sqrt{n}. Overall, from useful empirical observations it seems that the bounds proposed by the authors are tighter than these other bounds.
reviews.main_review:
There have been several works that generalize from gaussian settings to the more general exponential family, including the ones that are cited by the authors but also for example monotone retargetting and exponential family PCA. As such there are several tools for such an analysis that are pretty well known now. Other than that, the analysis follows closely with that of Li et al, with few minor but important differences. 

I am not sure if the extension to exponential family is a major contribution. It allows for analysis of signSGD. But given several other works of similar nature, I am not sure if the analysis and the major contribution as claimed, including in the title of the paper justifies a publication. 

It seems, however, that proposing gradient discrepancy itself is a useful contribution. Can the authors highlight the difference in the analysis from Li et al explicitly that led to this difference in the bounds ? Why is this sidelined as a side-effect? Does this follow from the new exponential family based analysis proposed here (it does not seem to be so) ? The experiments are also conducted based on this contribution. As such the main message/contribution of the paper and the empirical section seems disconnected. I am happy to be corrected.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The main contribution of exponential family dynamics seems not very significant. Gradient discrepancy is sidelined as a minor contribution but is focussed on a lot more in the empirical section, which makes the paper seems disconnected.

reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Paper Summary

This work establishes generalization bounds for stochastic gradient Langevin dynamics (SGLD) with noise distributions from the exponential family, extending beyond the conventional Gaussian noise framework. The authors demonstrate that their formulation encompasses standard Langevin dynamics as a special case and provide concrete applications to sign-based SGD. Rather than relying on gradient norm-based bounds (as in Li et al.) or gradient incoherence measures (as in Negrea et al., which scale as 1/√n), the authors introduce gradient discrepancy as an alternative complexity measure. Empirical observations suggest their proposed bounds achieve tighter guarantees compared to existing approaches.

## Detailed Comments

The literature on generalizing from Gaussian to exponential family settings is already well-established. Prior work such as monotone retargeting and exponential family PCA have developed and refined analytical techniques for such generalizations. Consequently, many of the tools employed in this analysis are now relatively standard in the field. The technical approach largely mirrors that of Li et al., with only modest but noteworthy modifications distinguishing the analysis.

I have reservations about whether the extension to exponential family noise represents a sufficiently substantial contribution on its own merit. While this generalization does enable analysis of signSGD, the landscape of similar generalization results is already populated with comparable work. Given the existing body of literature, it is unclear whether the claimed significance of this extension—particularly as emphasized in the paper's title—warrants publication at a top venue.

That said, the introduction of gradient discrepancy as an alternative complexity measure appears to constitute a valuable contribution in its own right. However, the paper presents this in a manner that obscures its importance. Specifically, the authors should provide an explicit and detailed explanation of how their analysis diverges from Li et al.'s approach and what aspects of this divergence led to the improved bound. Why is this conceptual innovation treated as secondary rather than positioned as a primary contribution? Moreover, it remains unclear whether this improvement naturally emerges from the exponential family framework or represents an independent insight. The empirical validation, notably, centers on evaluating this gradient discrepancy measure, yet this disconnect between the theoretical narrative and experimental focus creates an impression of misalignment in the paper's overall message.

## Summary

The claimed primary contribution regarding exponential family dynamics appears limited in scope relative to existing work. Conversely, gradient discrepancy emerges as a genuinely useful contribution but is underemphasized in the theoretical presentation, while simultaneously dominating the experimental section. This structural mismatch undermines the paper's coherence and impact.

## Recommendation

**Rating: 5 (marginally below the acceptance threshold)**

The paper makes incremental theoretical contributions but lacks sufficient novelty in its main claims. The genuine insights regarding gradient discrepancy require better integration into the central narrative to strengthen the work's overall contribution and clarity.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper under review considers the generalized SDLG algorithms with exponential family noise introduced to the mini-batch gradient descent. The analysis of the error bound is based on the control of the expected stability. Experiments are provided to support the proposed EFLD SGD algorithms.
reviews.main_review:
Strength.
1. The paper proves the generalization error of ''existing'' noisy stochastic iterative algorithms to have a $\mathcal O(1/n)$ sample dependence. 
2. The exponential family noise is considered in the generalized SGD algorithms. The generalized error bound is based on expected stability, which seems to bring new ideas for general exponential family noise.  A thorough analysis of the error bound of exponential family noise SGD algorithms is provided. 

Weakness.

1. The proposed algorithm does not show state-of-the-art performance.  The ultimate goal of the proposed algorithms is to achieve better performance on real data sets. Without strong support from the empirical study,  it is not convincible to believe why one bothers to study such more complicated cases. 
2. Adding to the above point, the paper only reviews the SGD in the literature, there have been very recent works based on Langevin dynamics, e.g. replica-exchange Langevin dynamic SGD, second-order Hamiltonian Langevin dynamics. Those algorithms achieve state-of-the-art performance. It is questionable to ask if the newly proposed algorithms in this paper could provide better performance if the model architectures are improved.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper seems to provide a new family of SGD algorithms. The potential of the model seems to be broad. The theoretical analysis is recognized and deserves their own interest. However, the current state of the empirical study does not show promising performance.

Summary Of The Paper:
The paper under review considers the generalized SDLG algorithms with exponential family noise introduced to the mini-batch gradient descent. The analysis of the error bound is based on the control of the expected stability. Experiments are provided to support the proposed EFLD SGD algorithms.

Main Review:
Strength.
1. The paper proves the generalization error of ''existing'' noisy stochastic iterative algorithms to have a $\mathcal O(1/n)$ sample dependence. 
2. The exponential family noise is considered in the generalized SGD algorithms. The generalized error bound is based on expected stability, which seems to bring new ideas for general exponential family noise.  A thorough analysis of the error bound of exponential family noise SGD algorithms is provided. 

Weakness.

1. The proposed algorithm does not show state-of-the-art performance.  The ultimate goal of the proposed algorithms is to achieve better performance on real data sets. Without strong support from the empirical study,  it is not convincible to believe why one bothers to study such more complicated cases. 
2. Adding to the above point, the paper only reviews the SGD in the literature, there have been very recent works based on Langevin dynamics, e.g. replica-exchange Langevin dynamic SGD, second-order Hamiltonian Langevin dynamics. Those algorithms achieve state-of-the-art performance. It is questionable to ask if the newly proposed algorithms in this paper could provide better performance if the model architectures are improved.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper seems to provide a new family of SGD algorithms. The potential of the model seems to be broad. The theoretical analysis is recognized and deserves their own interest. However, the current state of the empirical study does not show promising performance.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary of the Paper

This manuscript introduces a family of generalized stochastic descent algorithms that incorporate exponential family noise distributions into mini-batch gradient descent frameworks. The theoretical analysis employs expected stability as the primary tool for deriving error bounds, and empirical validation is provided through computational experiments on the proposed exponential family-based SGD variants.

## Strengths

1. The work establishes that generalization error bounds for noisy stochastic iterative algorithms exhibit $\mathcal{O}(1/n)$ sample complexity, extending results to existing algorithm classes.

2. The incorporation of exponential family noise into generalized SGD represents a meaningful theoretical contribution. The derivation of generalization bounds through the lens of expected stability offers a novel analytical framework that appears well-suited for handling the broader class of exponential family distributions. The paper presents a comprehensive theoretical treatment of these more general noise models in the SGD setting.

## Weaknesses

1. The empirical results fail to demonstrate competitive performance relative to existing methods. Given that the ultimate practical motivation for studying these algorithms should be improved real-world performance, the experimental validation is insufficient to justify the added algorithmic complexity. The computational studies do not provide convincing evidence for why practitioners should adopt these approaches.

2. Furthermore, the paper's literature review is limited primarily to standard SGD variants. The field has recently seen significant advances through Langevin dynamics-based approaches, including techniques such as replica-exchange Langevin dynamic SGD and second-order Hamiltonian Langevin dynamics, which have demonstrated superior empirical performance. It remains unclear whether the proposed algorithms could achieve competitive results if combined with improved model architectures, or whether they offer inherent advantages over these more sophisticated existing methods.

## Summary

While the paper presents a theoretically interesting generalization of SGD algorithms to exponential family noise settings, with solid theoretical analysis that merits independent consideration, the practical impact remains questionable. The experimental evaluation does not demonstrate sufficient empirical advantages to support the increased theoretical complexity introduced by the proposed framework.

## Rating

**5: marginally below the acceptance threshold**
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper under review considers the generalized SDLG algorithms with exponential family noise introduced to the mini-batch gradient descent. The analysis of the error bound is based on the control of the expected stability. Experiments are provided to support the proposed EFLD SGD algorithms.

Main Review:
Strength.
1. The paper proves the generalization error of ''existing'' noisy stochastic iterative algorithms to have a $\mathcal O(1/n)$ sample dependence. 
2. The exponential family noise is considered in the generalized SGD algorithms. The generalized error bound is based on expected stability, which seems to bring new ideas for general exponential family noise.  A thorough analysis of the error bound of exponential family noise SGD algorithms is provided. 

Weakness.

1. The proposed algorithm does not show state-of-the-art performance.  The ultimate goal of the proposed algorithms is to achieve better performance on real data sets. Without strong support from the empirical study,  it is not convincible to believe why one bothers to study such more complicated cases. 
2. Adding to the above point, the paper only reviews the SGD in the literature, there have been very recent works based on Langevin dynamics, e.g. replica-exchange Langevin dynamic SGD, second-order Hamiltonian Langevin dynamics. Those algorithms achieve state-of-the-art performance. It is questionable to ask if the newly proposed algorithms in this paper could provide better performance if the model architectures are improved.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper seems to provide a new family of SGD algorithms. The potential of the model seems to be broad. The theoretical analysis is recognized and deserves their own interest. However, the current state of the empirical study does not show promising performance.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Learning Visual-Linguistic Adequacy, Fidelity, and Fluency for Novel Object Captioning

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes a framework that combines masked language models (BERT) and image-text embedding models (CLIP) for novel object captioning. The proposed model significantly improves the fluency, fidelity, and adequacy of the generated images. Extensive experiments are conducted and the comparison between the proposed model and existing models shows that the proposed model performs relatively well.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:

S1: This paper pays attention to three important properties of captions -- fluency, fidelity and adequacy and proposes a formwork to generate fluent, faithful and adequate captions to describe images.

S2: Extensive experimental results show that the proposed model outperforms existing models on novel object captioning.

Weaknesses:

W1: The proposed model combines some existing models, like BERT, CLIP, so one of my concerns is the novelty of the proposed framwork. Plus, the reward to train the model is not novel, which is used in Rotian's work [1] and distilling from BERT for text generation was proposed by Chen [2]. Also, the observation that repeating the objects that occur in an image can improve retrieval score is not new [3].

W2: Neigher BERT nor CLIP is trained using the dataset used in the paper, so how to mitigate the problem of domain shift when applying BERT and CLIP? I do not think you can assume that BERT and CLIP are always correct.

W3: It seems that BERT is only used during training. I am wondering whether BERT can be used in the inference stage, for example, using the captioning model to generate a caption with beam search and then employing BERT to generate more captions. In this way, we can see whether BERT can improve fluency.

W4: Lack of human study.

[1] Rotian Luo et al.. Discriminability objective for training descriptive captions. CVPR, 2018.

[2] Y-C Chen et al.. Distilling Knowledge Learned in BERT for Text Generation. ACL, 2020.

[3] Qingzhong Wang et al.. Describing Like Humans: on Diversity in Image Captioning. CVPR, 2019.

reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary of the Paper

The authors present an approach that integrates masked language models (BERT) with vision-language embedding models (CLIP) to address the task of novel object captioning. The proposed framework demonstrates improvements across multiple dimensions of caption quality, including fluency, fidelity, and adequacy. The paper includes comprehensive experimental validation comparing the proposed approach against several baseline methods, with results indicating competitive or superior performance.

## Main Review

### Strengths

**S1**: The paper identifies and explicitly targets three crucial dimensions of caption quality—fluency, fidelity, and adequacy—and develops a framework specifically designed to optimize performance across these metrics.

**S2**: The experimental evaluation is thorough and demonstrates that the proposed approach achieves superior results compared to existing methods in the novel object captioning task.

### Weaknesses

**W1**: A significant concern regarding this work is its limited technical novelty. The framework primarily assembles existing components (BERT and CLIP) without substantial innovation in their integration. Furthermore, the training objective itself lacks originality, as similar reward-based formulations have been previously explored in Rotian's work [1]. The strategy of extracting knowledge from BERT for text generation builds directly on prior work by Chen et al. [2]. Additionally, the observation that repeating objects present in the image can enhance retrieval performance is not a novel finding [3].

**W2**: Since neither BERT nor CLIP was pretrained on the dataset employed in this study, there remains an unresolved concern about domain adaptation. The paper does not adequately address how to handle potential domain shift when deploying these pretrained models. Assuming that BERT and CLIP will perform optimally without explicit domain-specific refinement may not be justified.

**W3**: The paper appears to restrict BERT's role to the training phase only. It would be valuable to explore whether BERT could also be leveraged during inference—for instance, by using the captioning model to generate initial captions via beam search, followed by BERT-based refinement to produce alternative captions. Such an approach could provide stronger evidence regarding BERT's contribution to improving caption fluency.

**W4**: The paper lacks human evaluation. Automated metrics alone are insufficient to fully validate the claimed improvements in caption quality.

## Summary of the Review

The paper presents a reasonable baseline approach for novel object captioning by combining BERT and CLIP. While the experimental results are solid, the contribution is primarily in the engineering of existing models rather than in methodological innovation.

## Rating

**6: marginally above the acceptance threshold**

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes to improve novel object captioning i.e. describing objects/contents that are not seen during training. Specifically, the authors propose to correct the captions generated from captioning models using pre-trained BERT and CLIP models by rewarding the captions with precise and rich visual content. They show qualitative and quantitative improvements on CiDER and SPICE scores compared to previous work and propose to measure the fluency, fidelity and adequacy of the generated captions for a more comprehensive evaluation of novel object captioning.
reviews.main_review:
### Pros:
- Leveraging prior visual-linguistic knowledge from pre-trained BERT and CLIP models for the task of novel object captioning. The association between fidelity, fluency and adequacy with the training tasks of BERT and CLIP presents a good motivation for the method adopted. 

- The reward based loss functions designed for encouraging caption novelty and fluency and to discourage repetitive captions is interesting and novel. 

- The authors design fidelity/fluency and adequacy quantitative evaluation to measure the efficacy of their approach compared to previous work and show improvements. 

### Clarifications:
- Is the BERT model finetuned with the captions from the nocaps dataset in eq 2?  How is the CLIP condition satisfied in eq2, what happens if both the captions are unreliable ? Is there a threshold above which the CLIP scores are compared ?

- The BERT equations also could fulfil the fidelity objective because of the gating function. Is this observed?

- Are the improvements from Eq5. coming because of the r_CIDER term in the labelled caption data? Are the results in Table 1 compared with CIDER optimization for baselines? 

- What is observed if the reward is not calculated over the labeled training samples X_l?

- How will  the model perform if Eq 2 is performed during the inference stage only from a pre-trained captioning model?

- In fig 1, it is not very clear where the second caption “two glove hands...” comes from, is one from greedy decoding and other from sampling  ? 

- Are the novel words mostly objects/nouns?

### Cons: 

- While the method is effective and simple, there are certain ablations which could have been performed qualitatively and quantitatively to draw where the improvements come from. 

- The improvements in the method over the SOTA are via CIDER optimization on labeled examples in Eq 5. If the baselines are trained with CIDEr optimization, it might improve overall performance as well. 

- The motivation of improving fluency or adequacy is probably not only related to novel image captioning but for image captioning in general. Could this method help to address those problems there? 

- As stated in the paper, that previous methods produce grammatically incorrect captions for NOC, that does not seem to be the case (eg in fig 4). There are details missing from baseline captions but they are still fluent.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper is well written with detailed experimental results to support the claim. The paper proposes interesting ideas to improve NOC and is marginally novel but there are still some concerns as stated in the main review.

Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes to improve novel object captioning i.e. describing objects/contents that are not seen during training. Specifically, the authors propose to correct the captions generated from captioning models using pre-trained BERT and CLIP models by rewarding the captions with precise and rich visual content. They show qualitative and quantitative improvements on CiDER and SPICE scores compared to previous work and propose to measure the fluency, fidelity and adequacy of the generated captions for a more comprehensive evaluation of novel object captioning.

Main Review:
### Pros:
- Leveraging prior visual-linguistic knowledge from pre-trained BERT and CLIP models for the task of novel object captioning. The association between fidelity, fluency and adequacy with the training tasks of BERT and CLIP presents a good motivation for the method adopted. 

- The reward based loss functions designed for encouraging caption novelty and fluency and to discourage repetitive captions is interesting and novel. 

- The authors design fidelity/fluency and adequacy quantitative evaluation to measure the efficacy of their approach compared to previous work and show improvements. 

### Clarifications:
- Is the BERT model finetuned with the captions from the nocaps dataset in eq 2?  How is the CLIP condition satisfied in eq2, what happens if both the captions are unreliable ? Is there a threshold above which the CLIP scores are compared ?

- The BERT equations also could fulfil the fidelity objective because of the gating function. Is this observed?

- Are the improvements from Eq5. coming because of the r_CIDER term in the labelled caption data? Are the results in Table 1 compared with CIDER optimization for baselines? 

- What is observed if the reward is not calculated over the labeled training samples X_l?

- How will  the model perform if Eq 2 is performed during the inference stage only from a pre-trained captioning model?

- In fig 1, it is not very clear where the second caption “two glove hands...” comes from, is one from greedy decoding and other from sampling  ? 

- Are the novel words mostly objects/nouns?

### Cons: 

- While the method is effective and simple, there are certain ablations which could have been performed qualitatively and quantitatively to draw where the improvements come from. 

- The improvements in the method over the SOTA are via CIDER optimization on labeled examples in Eq 5. If the baselines are trained with CIDEr optimization, it might improve overall performance as well. 

- The motivation of improving fluency or adequacy is probably not only related to novel image captioning but for image captioning in general. Could this method help to address those problems there? 

- As stated in the paper, that previous methods produce grammatically incorrect captions for NOC, that does not seem to be the case (eg in fig 4). There are details missing from baseline captions but they are still fluent.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper is well written with detailed experimental results to support the claim. The paper proposes interesting ideas to improve NOC and is marginally novel but there are still some concerns as stated in the main review.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Peer Review

## Summary

This paper addresses the problem of novel object captioning—generating descriptions for objects not encountered during model training. The authors propose a correction mechanism for captions produced by existing captioning models, leveraging pre-trained BERT and CLIP models to enhance caption quality by rewarding visual-linguistic alignment. The work demonstrates quantitative gains on CiDER and SPICE metrics relative to prior approaches and introduces a three-dimensional evaluation framework encompassing fluency, fidelity, and adequacy metrics for more nuanced assessment of novel object captioning systems.

## Strengths

- The exploitation of pre-trained visual-linguistic representations from BERT and CLIP is well-motivated, with a clear connection between the training objectives of these models and the desired properties of fluency, fidelity, and adequacy in generated captions.

- The proposed reward-based loss formulations that simultaneously encourage novelty and fluency while penalizing repetitive patterns represent a meaningful contribution to the field.

- The introduction and quantitative measurement of fidelity, fluency, and adequacy provides a more granular evaluation framework, and the paper demonstrates measurable improvements across these dimensions relative to existing methods.

## Questions for Clarification

- Is the BERT model in Eq. 2 fine-tuned using captions from the nocaps dataset? How is the CLIP constraint enforced in Eq. 2, and what occurs when both candidate captions are unreliable? Is a threshold mechanism employed for comparing CLIP scores?

- Given the gating mechanism in the BERT formulation, could it also satisfy the fidelity objective? Is this phenomenon empirically observed in the results?

- Do the improvements reported in Eq. 5 primarily stem from the r_CIDER term applied to labeled data? Were baseline methods in Table 1 similarly optimized with CIDER-based objectives for fair comparison?

- What performance degradation is observed when reward computation excludes the labeled training set X_l?

- How does the model perform if the correction procedure in Eq. 2 is applied exclusively at inference time using a pre-trained captioning model?

- In Figure 1, the origin of the second caption ("two glove hands...") is unclear—does it come from greedy decoding versus sampling strategies?

- Are the novel words predominantly nouns and object references, or do they span other lexical categories?

## Weaknesses

- The paper lacks sufficient ablation studies to isolate and quantify the individual contributions of different components. Both qualitative and quantitative ablations would strengthen the analysis and clarify which design choices drive performance improvements.

- The performance gains appear to be substantially driven by CIDER optimization on labeled examples (Eq. 5). If baseline methods were similarly trained with CIDER-based optimization, their performance might improve comparably, potentially narrowing the gap. This raises questions about the fairness of the comparison.

- The motivation for improving fluency and adequacy extends beyond novel object captioning and applies broadly to image captioning. The paper would benefit from discussing whether the proposed approach generalizes to standard captioning tasks and whether it addresses fundamental limitations in general-purpose captioning systems.

- The claim that previous methods generate grammatically incorrect captions for novel objects is not consistently supported by the evidence presented. Figure 4 suggests that baseline captions, while less detailed, maintain grammatical correctness; the primary limitation appears to be missing semantic content rather than syntactic errors.

## Overall Assessment

The paper is clearly written with comprehensive experimental validation. The proposed approach is sensible and introduces interesting ideas for advancing novel object captioning. However, the work is only marginally novel, and several concerns remain regarding the experimental design, particularly around baseline comparison fairness and the need for more thorough ablation analysis. The contribution is meaningful but incremental, and key questions about the source of improvements and generalizability warrant further investigation.

## Recommendation

**6: marginally above the acceptance threshold**

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The papers presents an approach for the task of novel object captioning (NOC). NOC requires the model to describe novel objects appearing in test images that were not described in the training captions. The authors claim that existing methods are not good at the task because they don't tackle three concepts required to produce a good caption — 

1) Fluency: generating human-like captions, 

2) Fidelity: whether the caption is describing the novel object in the image or not, 

3) Adequacy: whether the caption covers all the salient visual concepts in the image. 

The authors propose a model Visual-Linguistic Adequacy, Fidelity, and Adequacy (VLAF2) to address these issues. The model utilizes BERT to sample different variations of the captions by substituting random words from the caption. It uses CLIP to assess which of these are more relevant to the image. The model also uses CLIP score and combine it with CIDEr based reward to further train the captioning model via reinforce. 

The model is evaluated on the nocaps benchmark on which it achieves improvements over existing sota methods like VINVL+VIVO. The authors also show that apart from captioning metrics, the model also does better on fluency (B@4, C), Fidelity (Precision), Adequacy(Recall).
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths

1. Overall, I concur with the key insight from the paper which is that models for NOC should tackle fluency, fidelity and adequacy. It has been observed in Agrawal et al 2019 (nocaps dataset) that methods suffer from these issues. Captions which are fluent often aren't accurate, and captions which are accurate often aren't fluent.
2. The authors take reasonable steps to fix these issues. Using a LM to fix fluency is a good idea. The authors address one of the main issues with such an approach — BERT like language models can produce fluent captions but it's hard to judge their accuracy. The authors use CLIP to score these and select the best method. Such an approach makes sense.
3. Another novel contribution in this work is using uncaptioned images (images in the OpenImages dataset which do not have associated training captions) during training. Since these don't have training captions, they modify the reward to use CLIP as well penalize repeated words. Indeed, they show that CLIP + repeated penalty is helpful for overall performance (Table 3).

### Weaknesses

1. One of the biggest and most important weakness in the paper is that the paper doesn't follow the guidelines from the nocaps paper. As mentioned in the nocaps paper (section 3.3), "Do not use additional paired image-caption data collected from humans. Improving evaluation scores by leveraging additional human-generated paired imagecaption data is antithetical to this benchmark – the only paired image-caption dataset that should be used is the COCO Captions 2017 training split. However, external text corpora, knowledge bases, and object detection datasets may be used during training or inference". The paper heavily relies on CLIP which has been trained on alt-text data for images (which are human written) for improving the captioning performance. This makes the method proposed in the paper not comparable to existing methods in my opinion. The authors should have used the nocaps (XD) for evaluation.
2. Related to the second point, I would have liked to see if you can replace CLIP with other approaches (ViLBERT) which also outputs a score indicating how well the caption is aligned with the image. This would form a fair comparison.
3. Since CLIP is trained on alt-text data for images, it would be interesting to see if joint-training on Conceptual Captions (or a similar web-scale data) works better instead.
4. CLIP is being used for checking the relevance of the caption with the image. I wonder how will other similar metrics for caption relevance (VIFIDEL) compare against scores from CLIP.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall, I think the paper presents an interesting insight (breaking down caption quality into fluency, adequacy and fidelity) and presents an approach which improves all the three aspects on the nocaps dataset. I raised a couple of concerns (use of CLIP which has been trained on human-generated alt-text data) which goes against the guidelines of the benchmark. It nullifies some of the claims in the paper and I'd be interested in hearing from the authors regarding that. I would have also liked to see comparison with other caption relevance metrics like VIFIDEL which would make the contributions stronger. Overall, I am borderline on this paper and will update my thoughts based on the author response.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 4: The contributions are significant, and do not exist in prior works.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.
